title: Web Docs feature derivation — placing the workbench surface against WE standards
summary: Prep research for decision #626 (stage 3 of the Web Docs feature-pipeline epic #623): classify each of the 20 features in the #625 matrix (src/_data/workbenchFeatures.json) against the live registry — reuse / extend / mint, and at what layer. Applying the fork-existence test (mandate nothing; a fork is real only when branches are mutually-exclusive end-states) collapses the surface to ~11 ratify-as-classified reuse/extend/out-of-scope placements, a small block-mint set (props-table / story-canvas / code-view, forced by 'runnable code → Block'), and just TWO genuine forks. Fork 1 — the component-metadata interchange: adopt Custom Elements Manifest (CEM; custom-elements.json, schema 2.1.0, consumed by api-viewer + Storybook + VS Code custom-data + linters off one manifest) as a new WE PROTOCOL [recommended — it is a manifest many independent vendors interoperate on, WE's exact litmus for a protocol (classification Q2), and WE already hosts this shape in the changelog-manifest protocol (protocols.json:94); reuses platform vocabulary, inherits the consumer ecosystem] vs mint a bespoke WE manifest format vs tooling-only with no registered entity. Note: WE has NO formats registry, so the matrix's format:* refs are dangling — the entity-kind is a real open call. Fork 2 — docs examples ↔ webcases: docs 'examples/stories' (and interaction-tests) ARE the webcases fixture artifact [recommended — one fixture serves conformance + docs, and the #426 ingestion adapters already land CSF stories in the webcases pivot WebCase={id,title,description,code}] vs a sibling docs-only 'examples' concept (honours separation bias but duplicates the fixture). Research reshaped the matrix: api-viewer proves component-isolation + autodocs + args are ONE capability fed by one CEM (not three new builds); Pagefind (static WASM build-time index, no SaaS) reframes 'search' from a mint to reuse-input-blocks + a build step; long-form docs reuse the 11ty/markdown pipeline + a block-embed directive, NOT the unregistered webediting project. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-webdocs-feature-derivation.md.

title: Workbench feature surface — capability × tool matrix for Web Docs
summary: The cross-tabbed feature surface (capability × tool) over the workbench/docs-tool landscape from #624 — stage 2 of the Web Docs feature-pipeline epic #623, raw material the derivation decision #626 classifies. Registry: src/_data/workbenchFeatures.json (20 features in 7 groups: render, authoring, controls, testing, theming, discovery, distribution). Each feature records which landscape tools ship it, ubiquity (table-stakes→niche), a one-line 'what a WE-native version would need', and the load-bearing weStatus column that pre-classifies it for #626: reuse-existing / extend-existing / new-surface / out-of-scope. The decisive finding: roughly two thirds of the surface already maps to standards WE has. REUSE-EXISTING — viewport→breakpoint intent, theming + token-tables→webtheme(+data-table), a11y panel→webcompliance, navigation/IA→nav-list/tree-select/breadcrumb, i18n→webintl, analytics→webanalytics, access-control/metered→access-control+web-identity (this is what powers #398 open-core tiering), extensibility→webplugs/webregistries. EXTEND-EXISTING — args/controls→Technical Configurator fed by CEM, stories/states + interaction-tests→webcases overlap, long-form docs→webediting authoring, doc versioning→upgrader/webadapters. GENUINELY NEW surface (the small core #626 must actually design) — the component-isolation renderer, CEM-driven autodocs (the #624 emit prior; api-viewer is the zero-build proof), full-text search index, source/code view. OUT-OF-SCOPE for the standard — visual regression (a CI/hosted service → #398 hosted tier, Chromatic-paid vs Lost-Pixel-OSS) and the Figma design↔code bridge (design-tool integration; its fully-hosted form, Backlight, already failed). Net: #626 is mostly a dedupe-and-compose exercise, not a greenfield mint. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-component-workbench-landscape.md (landscape) + this matrix.

title: Component-workbench / docs-tool landscape — Storybook and its peers
summary: The consolidated inventory of Storybook-like tools that document component libraries (backlog #624, first stage of the Web Docs feature-pipeline epic #623). Until now only Storybook + Mintlify were named (as ingest targets in #426); this enumerates the whole landscape across five axes so the feature-surface sweep (#625) and the derive-to-WE-standards decision (#626) have a complete, reproducible source list. The five axes: (1) local OSS workbenches — Storybook (category anchor; v9 folded test/a11y/visual into core), Histoire (Vite-native, pre-v1 one-maintainer caution), Ladle (reads CSF directly), React Styleguidist + Pattern Lab (both retiring; their ideas — docs-from-source, Atomic Design taxonomy — survive the tools). (2) docs platforms — Docusaurus, Nextra, Starlight (native-first, near-zero JS — the philosophical model for a WE-native surface) vs hosted Mintlify, GitBook, ReadMe. (3) hosted design-system catalogs — Zeroheight, Supernova, Knapsack, Bit; Backlight RETIRED (divRIOTS shut it 1 Jun 2025 — signal that the fully-hosted browser design-system-IDE model did not endure). (4) visual-test/publish — Chromatic, Percy, Applitools vs OSS self-hostable Lost Pixel. (5) machine-readable inventory formats — Custom Elements Manifest (CEM), CSF3, web-types, api-viewer, react-docgen. Two load-bearing priors handed to #626: (a) CEM is the format Web Docs should EMIT — web-component-native declarative JSON already consumed by Storybook-for-web-components, api-viewer (a single zero-build web component that renders CEM as live docs+playground — the closest analog to a WE-native docs element), and IDEs; CSF3/react-docgen are ingest sources, not emit targets; (b) the open-core seam is market-validated — every commercial tier (Chromatic, Zeroheight, Mintlify-hosted) has an OSS/self-hostable counterpart (Lost Pixel, Docusaurus/Starlight, Storybook), exactly the free-OSS-workbench + paid-hosted-conveniences shape #398 plans. This corpus is complementary to benchmarkCorpus.json: that is the design systems Web Docs renders, this is the tools Web Docs competes with and ingests from. Registry in src/_data/workbenchTools.json; full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-component-workbench-landscape.md.

title: Reveal navigation menus — the disclosure-panel paradigm (mega-menu / hover-disclosure)
summary: Grounds decision #609: top-level nav whose sections disclose a panel of links on hover/focus (dropdown nav, mega-menus, the WE header). The survey is decisive that this is the WAI-ARIA APG *Disclosure Navigation* pattern (native list/anchor semantics + aria-expanded + aria-controls) composed with the existing Anchor intent (placement/collision/strategy/dismissal) — NOT the menu/menubar role (composite-widget roving focus + first-character type-ahead, for application command menus like Google Docs), and NOT a new widget. The base already exists as the nav-list block (blocks.json:2082, APG Disclosure Navigation, nav:section toggle). After research only TWO genuine forks survive. Fork 1 (home): a composition recipe (nav-list section + disclosure + anchor strategy=escape popover), no new block — DEFAULT, vs a named composition block matching the menu/drawer house-style precedent (both are 'composition manifests'); the mega-menu multi-column shape is demoted to support-all (panel layout, not a standard). Fork 2 (hover-intent home): the open/close-delay + safe-area corridor that fixes the diagonal problem (origin Tognazzini/Batson Apple HID, the 'Amazon triangle') recurs across reveal-nav, menu submenus, and hovercards — so per the separation bias mint a small standalone hover-intent behavioral concern they compose (DEFAULT) vs fold a hoverIntent dimension into the anchor intent; the MECHANIC (delay-only à la Radix/Headless · CSS safe-area bridge à la the WE dogfood · JS pointer-direction polygon à la jQuery-menu-aim/safe-triangle — unsettled across libraries, Radix ships delay-only with open polygon requests #1549/#2437) is a Technical Configurator setting, not a UX dimension or WE mandate. DISSOLVED by research: 'no-reflow' is the existing anchor.strategy=inline|escape axis (in-flow sidebar accordion reflows fine; header popover must not), not a new dimension; i18n label-growth (no English-keyed fixed widths, no truncation) and native-first CSS-disclosure-baseline-plus-JS-enhancement are forced invariants; disclosure-not-menu is a forced invariant (ratify). The dogfood WE header (base.njk:32-69, style.css:241-276) is the conform-to-standard reference and is NOT yet APG-conformant (heads are aria-hidden spans, no aria-expanded/controls, hover/:focus-within-only with no Esc/outside-click) — conforming it is a graduation build, not a fork. Both forks confidence: med. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-reveal-navigation-disclosure-pattern.md.

title: Spec vs. reference runtime vs. polyfill — who owns the executable substrate of a standard
summary: Grounds decision #606: does the `plugs/` runtime (registries + browser-API patches that make WE standards runnable — `plugs/webregistries/`, `plugs/webinjectors/`, `plugs/bootstrap.ts`) belong in the standard layer (Web Everything), the impl layer (Frontier UI), or its own published package? The prior-art survey reduces the call to ONE archetype question: which of three real ecosystem patterns is `plugs/`? (1) POLYFILL — spec != runtime, the runtime lives OUTSIDE the spec: Web Components polyfills ship as @webcomponents/webcomponentsjs in a separate repo, core-js is a third-party ECMAScript polyfill TC39 does NOT publish (TC39 ships the spec + the test262 conformance suite, tests not runtime) -> pushes plugs out of WE. (2) REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION — a canonical executable embodiment kept WITH the spec to make it demonstrable/testable: CommonMark ships the spec AND cmark under one org; dart-sass is the canonical Sass impl; CPython is the reference PEPs realize against -> keeps the runtime with the standard. (3) SHARED SUBSTRATE PACKAGE — a primitive factored into its own package consumed by MANY higher layers: React's scheduler/react-reconciler shared by react-dom/react-native; @lit/reactive-element under LitElement -> earned by PLURALITY of consumers. The two are not mutually exclusive (TC39 ships tests-not-runtime; CommonMark ships both), so 'specs ship no runtime' is a tendency of mature multi-vendor standards, not a law binding a young standard whose reference runtime IS its demonstration vehicle. Load-bearing classification: WE's plugs are a REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION (the runtime that makes the standard runnable in a browser; demos exercise the standard itself) — Frontier UI's blocks/ are the application implementations on top, correctly outside the standard. The #239 reading in the item was incomplete: #239 re-scoped jsx-runtime to @frontierui DESPITE no @frontierui import, purely because 'it IS implementation' — so #239's principle is standard-artifact-vs-implementation, NOT published-vs-unpublished; the unpublished-alias argument is a red herring, and a reference impl is a standard artifact #239 does not push out. Recommendation: A (WE owns the reference runtime) on the corrected reference-impl justification; C (carve a standalone webplugs package) is the named escape hatch the separation bias triggers the moment a SECOND independent impl consumes plugs (today FU is the only impl consumer; plateau-app consumes the same alias as a product, not an impl, so the split is not yet earned); B (FU owns it) rejected — no archetype supports the impl layer owning the standard's own reference runtime, and it inverts the demo model. One fork at DoR, confidence med-high. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-plugs-runtime-ownership-boundary.md.

title: General reference-retirement convention — generalize the corpus `retired` shape across all reference homes
summary: Grounds decision #584 (under epic #583, the reference-health dogfood layer): generalize #546's corpus `retired`/`retiredDate`/`retiredReason` (keep-not-delete) shape across every place the project cites an EXTERNAL reference. Inventory finds two clusters — structured JSON homes with external URLs that are the convention's real target (benchmarkCorpus.json `docsUrl`/`repoUrl`, the only home that already has `retired`; references.json `links[].url`; blocks.json/intents.json `designSystemResearch[].reference`; benchmarkCapabilityPresence.json `rows[].url`) and freeform prose homes (reports/*.md, research-descriptions/*.njk) where no field-set is enforceable. researchTopics.json already carries the REPLACEMENT half of the model (`supersededBy` + `lastReviewed`/`reviewHorizon`, #192/#441/#477/#478); crossRef cites only internal links; adapters/protocols cite no external URLs yet. Validation today is one rule (warn on a `verified` presence row lacking `url`, check-standards-rules.mjs:740); no JSON Schema files exist — validation is imperative. Prior-art survey is decisive on two points: (1) the retirement vocabulary is document-type-agnostic — W3C applies the same lifecycle states to a REC, a Note, a decision; IETF the same headers to every RFC — so homes differ in CONTAINER not in the retirement CONCEPT (argues uniform shape over per-home). (2) Death and supersession are distinct, linkable states, not one axis — W3C separates superseded (newer recommended, old still valid) from obsolete (no longer used) from rescinded (withdrawn); IETF mirrors with bidirectional Obsoletes:/Updates: headers PLUS a separate Historic status; PEP `Superseded-By:`, ADR `Supersedes/SupersededBy` carry only the replacement pointer; JSON Schema/OpenAPI `deprecated:true` is the minimal keep-not-delete annotation. This dissolves the original 'how does it mesh with #192's supersedes chain?' fork into a concrete choice: two orthogonal markers (a `retired` triplet for death + reuse #192's `supersededBy` pointer for replacement — a ref can be both, as FAST is) vs one richer W3C-style `status` enum. Classification: data governance not a WE standard (the platform setting is #583 layer 2); 7-question layer pass N/A; binding principles are native-first vocabulary reuse, reproducibility under #192 (keep-not-delete), bias-toward-separation (orthogonal markers), most-permissive default (opt-in metadata). Four forks at DoR: uniform shape; one documented convention + one shared validator helper; two orthogonal markers reusing supersededBy; structured homes now with freeform extraction deferred to #597. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-reference-retirement-convention.md.

title: Web Docs incumbent-ingestion adapters — Storybook (CSF) & Mintlify to the webcases pivot
summary: Prior-art survey + architecture prep for decision #426 (the adapter home/import fork that governs every webdocs ingestion adapter — #426 Storybook, #429 Mintlify, future Docusaurus), under epic #398 (Web Docs product). A webdocs ingestion adapter is a bottom-up normalization-hub shim (the #150 lossy-pivot direction): read an existing component-docs source and emit the webcases pivot the #424 generator consumes — WebCase = {id,title,description,code}, WebCases = Record<blockId, WebCase[]> (webdocs/generator.ts:27-46). Storybook (CSF 3): one Meta default export + N named Story exports per *.stories.* module; id ← story export key / @storybook/csf toId(); title ← story name or startCase(exportKey); description ← JSDoc above the story / parameters.docs.description.story; code ← verbatim story source or parameters.docs.source.code (the runtime 'dynamic' snippet is framework-specific, post-render — prefer the literal). Lossy/no-equivalent: args/argTypes control matrices, play interaction tests, decorators (so code isn't self-contained), multi-framework renderings. Reuse @storybook/csf-tools (readCsf/loadCsf → CsfFile.parse(), Storybook's own indexer) + @storybook/csf (toId, storyNameFromExport); .mdx docs via @mdx-js/mdx + remark-mdx. Mintlify: docs.json nav config + *.mdx pages with YAML frontmatter; id ← MDX path/slug, group ← navigation.groups[].group; title/description ← frontmatter; code ← fenced blocks / <CodeGroup> / <RequestExample>. Lossy: interactive OpenAPI playground, component embeds (<Card>/<Tabs>/<Steps>), nav hierarchy, multi-language CodeGroup sets, prose body. Reuse gray-matter + @mdx-js/mdx + remark + unist-util-visit; JSON.parse for docs.json. Cross-cutting: both sources are page/module-grained not case-grained (CSF fans out one-case-per-story naturally; Mintlify needs a per-page-vs-per-snippet granularity choice); the lossy cells ARE the normalization-hub's comparative value — flag, never silently drop. Two forks brought to DoR: (Fork 1, adapter home) co-locate in webeverything/webdocs/adapters/ next to the pivot [recommended — matches the in-repo ingest precedent scripts/validation-normalize/adapters/eslint.mjs, both adapter families live in webeverything, FUI has no @we/webdocs reach: tsconfig.json:20-26 / vite.config.mts:126-132] vs adapters in FUI which first requires exposing the pivot [honors the literal 'adapters→FUI' line of the #091 layering ruling but splits the generator's input-shim across a repo boundary]; (Fork 2, contract shape) a plain per-source provider module {source, ingest} → WebCases [recommended — author-time devtools provider seam per the runtime-DI-vs-provider rule, NOT a CustomXRegistry] vs a CustomWebcasesAdapterRegistry [cargo-cult: no runtime consultation]. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-13-webdocs-ingestion-adapter-home.md.

title: On-device UI-screenshot vision — capability ceiling, runtimes, distillation (a moving target)
summary: Capability grounding for backlog #488 — whether Plateau backs its vision features with an OWNED on-device model (fixed cost → flat-rate-safe under the linear-cost-with-revenue rule) rather than only a metered hosted SDK. The decisive finding: the gate verdict is COARSE screen classification, not generative understanding — Enrico (a Rico subset) shows the screenshot alone classifies UI screens at ~95% AUC / 75% top-1 across 20 design topics with a LIGHTWEIGHT classifier (random=5%), and our task is only 6 classes, so a small ViT/MobileNet/ResNet-18 (≲10MB quantized) runs in-browser via WebGPU on ANY device — true zero-marginal-cost — and is distillable from the big-model verdicts the dev gate already produces (VL2Lite CVPR'25, PAND, VLM-KD are established task-specific KD methods; #489 collects the labeled set). A richer tier (tagging, element/region detection, structure) needs a small on-device VLM — SmolVLM (256M/500M/2.2B), Moondream (0.5B/1.86B), Florence-2 (0.27B), TinyClick — at the practical ≲2B device ceiling (WebGPU ~2GB GPU mem, q4/q8, flagship mobile), with structure recovery still hard (Design2Code SOTA ≈76). Architecturally it's a provider swap behind the existing #475/#480 swappable vision seam, so the no-leakage invariant holds. THREE FRAMING PRINCIPLES (author steers): (1) the AI frontier moves fast — what's out of reach today may be feasible in months — so set graduation BENCHMARKS each tier must hit to move on-device (API/BYO-key as the always-on bridge meanwhile), don't permanently reject a feature; (2) RE-ASSESS on a cadence (a recurring re-benchmark of the small-model frontier, the #192 longitudinal model); (3) CODIFY THE TRAINING as a model-agnostic, re-applicable artifact — the labeled corpus + the distillation recipe + the benchmark suite are the durable assets, so switching base model means re-running the recipe, never re-labelling from scratch (minimize-lock-in applied to the model itself).

title: Backlog taxonomy — one `kind` axis vs separate `type` + `workItem`
summary: Grounds backlog decision #466 — whether to collapse the backlog's two near-redundant nature/sizing axes (`type`: idea/issue/decision + `workItem`: story/epic/task) into a single `kind` enum. Surveys how mature trackers separate the orthogonal concerns of nature, hierarchy-role, and effort: GitHub Issues (GA March 2025) converged on ONE org-level issue-type axis (Feature/Bug/Task) and explicitly told teams to migrate bug/feature *labels* INTO types — never a parallel required nature field; Jira runs ONE issue-type enum that folds hierarchy-role (Epic) and nature (Bug) together, with story points as a fully separate estimation field; Linear/GitLab likewise keep exactly one structural type axis plus a separate weight/estimate and free-form labels. None run WE's current double-statement (a buildable item is both `type:idea` AND `workItem:story`). The cross-tool invariant: collapse the correlated nature+role pair into one structural `kind` axis, keep the genuinely-orthogonal `size` separate, and demote any residual fix-vs-feature flavour to an optional label/tag rather than a required field. This confirms and strengthens #466's Fork-A lean (unify) from 'lean' to 'strong', and dissolves the would-be fix/feature sub-fork — the existing `tags` array already absorbs it with zero new machinery. Internal backlog tooling; no project-facing standard or lock-in.

title: Design-reference vision pipeline — capture-time QC gate + codification (one swappable provider)
summary: Grounds the two vision items on the design-reference corpus (backlog #475 capture-QC gate, #396 codification) on one swappable vision provider used at two points. The phase-1 pipeline (#382) admits screenshots through a deterministic, hand-authored readySelector gate that doesn't generalise and can't tell a clean app surface from a marketing splash / modal-obstructed view / stale error panel (Photopea & Grafana slipped through). The survey of UI vision prior art finds an asymmetry that decides the architecture: coarse screen classification is a mature, reliable VLM capability (ScreenAI's element-type+location annotation, Ferret-UI's widget classification beating GPT-4V), while full screenshot→structure recovery is still hard (Design2Code NAACL-2025 SOTA: Claude-4-Sonnet 76.3, open VLMs <45, poor layout fidelity). So the cheap admit/remediate/quarantine verdict (#475) is safe to automate as a VLM-as-judge returning a structured verdict against fixed criteria, while the rich tag+paradigm-harvest pass (#396) stays human-in-the-loop with the neutral structure as the editable review surface (the #086 ruling). Both are analysis calls, so they share ONE customVisionProvider registry (the #086 customMockupAnalyzerRegistry pattern) with two methods — not two integrations; obstructed-surface remediation reuses DuckDuckGo's Playwright-native autoconsent rather than inventing overlay dismissal; verdicts cache by content hash for idempotent re-runs. Internal research-corpus tooling, zero project-facing lock-in; deterministic selectors stay a free fast-path.

title: Custom Event Scoping & Registry
summary: Exploring the gap between Web Everything's scoping discipline (injectors, registries, dynamic block names) and the globally-scoped nature of DOM CustomEvent names.

title: DOM-Less Composition Patterns
summary: Researching every programming pattern and runtime solution that enables React-like component wrapping without adding DOM elements — from comment-based markers to display:contents, transient elements, and functional components.

title: Internationalization & Translation
summary: Surveying what major frameworks provide for i18n and translation that is not yet part of web standards — from message formatting and locale negotiation to compile-time translations and type-safe keys.

title: Virtual Elements: Directives or Components?
summary: Analyzing whether virtual elements (comment-based DOM-less markers) belong in Web Directives or Web Components, how they relate to transient elements, and when JSX makes transient syntax redundant.

title: Configurable Loading Threshold via Intents
summary: Researching how the 400ms debounce loading threshold should be configurable through the Intent system, backed by UX perception research and framework precedent.

title: Scoped Event Discrimination for Resource Loaders
summary: Analyzing how listenForScoped discriminates between concurrent resource loaders in the same scope, the relationship between loader events and tracing, and how trace context flows through resource requests.

title: Customizable Fetcher & Cache for Resource Loader
summary: Documenting how the resource-loader's transport (fetch by default) and cache should be injectable via the injector system, enabling projects to swap in axios, GraphQL clients, or custom cache implementations.

title: UI Configuration & Visual Variation
summary: Exploring how an HTML-first, standards-based component library can handle visual variations of the same core component — injecting icons, badges, popovers, or different visual treatments — without rebuilding accessibility logic. Catalogs strategies from React composition to web component slots and headless UI patterns.

title: Provide/Consume/Injector Syntax Proposals
summary: Cataloging the four layers of provide/consume/injector syntax explored in the plateau project — from runtime API (Consumable, consume(), Node context methods) through HTML declarative forms (script type=expression/context), decorators (@provide, @context), and language-level DSL (injector blocks, provide/consume/map statements). Evaluates what should carry forward to Web Everything and what was exploratory.

title: Web Injectors: Plateau Migration Gaps
summary: Tracking features present in the plateau injector system that are missing or incomplete in Web Everything's webinjectors — Consumable class, Node context methods, scoped createElement, cloneNode preservation, CustomContext auto-attachment, ModuleInjector, provider replacement, and Window/ShadowRoot entry points.

title: Injector Syntax: Draft Specification
summary: Formal draft specification for the injector syntax system — provide, consume, and injector block declarations. Defines three conformance tiers (Runtime API, TypeScript API, DSL), maps every DSL construct to an unplugged runtime equivalent, addresses both module-scope and DOM-scope injection, and documents tooling requirements for esbuild, TypeScript, Chrome DevTools, and transpiler plugins.

title: Plateau Migration: Outstanding TODOs Across All Modules
summary: Comprehensive catalog of all TODO, stub, and placeholder code remaining from the plateau migration across every module — webregistries, webinjectors, webcontexts, webcomponents, and core utilities. Tracks what's genuinely incomplete vs outdated comments, and identifies the highest-impact items for completion.

title: Validation as Composition of Meta-Intents
summary: Discovering that validation's core concerns (async lifecycle, severity vocabulary, error handling) are universal patterns already in the repo (Loader, Feedback, Message, Reliability). Validation composes these shared metas rather than redefining them—a pattern applicable to any standard with optional features.

title: Volar & TypeScript 7 (Corsa/tsgo) Compatibility
summary: Researching how Volar.js and Vue language tools will interact with TypeScript 7's native Go rewrite (Project Corsa / tsgo). Covers the Corsa IPC-based API surface, the loss of TSServer plugin support, the viability of writing Volar language plugins in Go or native code, performance profiles and bottlenecks of Volar-based language servers, and potential IPC/WASM/FFI communication patterns between native analysis engines and Volar's Node.js-based framework.

title: Error Recovery & Retry Patterns
summary: Framework and library patterns for retry strategies, circuit breakers, offline queuing, and HTTP error classification. Covers TanStack Query, axios-retry, urql retryExchange, SWR, and Apollo RetryLink — analyzing their retry configuration models, backoff algorithms, and offline handling. Grounds the Web Reliability standard's design decisions: why first-that-accepts over a chain object, why null-means-decline, and which patterns are HTTP-specific vs. universally applicable.

title: Change Tracking as Observability
summary: Cataloging every client-side change-tracking strategy — snapshot+diff, dirty-checking, Proxy interception (Vue 3/MobX/Valtio), signals/observers (TC39 Signals, SolidJS), Immer/Mutative structural-sharing patches, JSON Patch (RFC 6902), JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7386), event sourcing, CRDTs (Yjs/Automerge), Operational Transformation, MutationObserver, accessor tracking, polling — as a facet of observability rather than a form concern. Defines the classification axes (granularity, eagerness, representation, attribution, reversibility, transport, soundness, cost), sketches a provisional canonical change record and an interop/configuration layer that lets one app pick a preferred strategy and bridge several at once, and generalizes the validation customValidation:change-detection concern. Grounded in primary sources (RFC 6902/7386, TC39 Signals, Shapiro et al. CRDT survey, W3C Trace Context) via an adversarially-verified deep-research pass.

title: Collection Operations: Filter / Sort / Page / Group
summary: Resolving gap #10 — the data operations on collections (filter, sort, page, group) that no current intent owns. type-ahead is focus-movement, windowed-collection is rendering-which-items, loader is async fetch; none transform the dataset. Surveys the native semantics anchors that DO exist (aria-sort's ascending/descending/none/other state model, Intl.Collator for locale-aware comparison, the stable Array.prototype.sort comparator, the Open UI <th sortable> proposal) versus the absence of any native operation API, and the headless-table precedent (TanStack Table, AG Grid, MUI DataGrid) that models the four ops as an ordered, composable row-model pipeline (filter → sort → group → paginate). Decided 2026-06-03: a single, UX-only Collection Operations Intent whose dimensions are the four pipeline stages with a configurable order and sort given a structured sub-shape (key(s), direction, collation/case/numeric preference, empty-value placement); ambient defaults (collation/locale preference, page size) ride the intent DI channel, structural choices (sortable columns, multi-sort) ride trait selection. The intent carries no technical references — the technical sorting strategies (default sort, Intl.Collator, numeric comparator, decorate-sort-undecorate, server delegation) with their advantages/disadvantages are materialised as a Sorting Strategy domain in Plateau's Technical Configurator; server-side execution is documented as front-end-out-of-scope.

title: Navigation Intent Vocabulary: Five Orthogonal Dimensions
summary: The Navigation Intent was the thinnest in the catalog — a single structure axis (hierarchical/lateral/linear) — while the Router block already shipped scroll, history, transition, and leave-guard behaviors with no UX vocabulary to name them. This research holds the intent to the standard intent bar (user-perceivable, closed 2–4 value set, orthogonal to structure, web-standards grounded) and adds four dimensions plus one softer fifth: history (push/replace/none, Navigation API navigate({history})), scroll (auto/top/preserve/manual, NavigateEvent.intercept({scroll}) + History.scrollRestoration), transition (directional/fade/none — direction only, deferring feel to the motion intent because browser-initiated Back carries no View-Transition type), guard (free/confirm/blocked, Navigation intercept/preventDefault + beforeunload), and persistence (url/memory/session, the WHATWG URL-vs-History-state split — the deep-linkability choice most relevant to tabs and wizards). Establishes the composition boundary: prefetch eagerness stays in the prefetch intent, animation feel stays in motion, focus-on-route-change is referenced (APG: move to main heading) not owned and overlaps focus-delegation, and aria-current is derived from structure rather than a separable axis. Nested routing, base path, router mode, 404 fallback, and cross-document execution are classed implementation/config, not UX dimensions. Every added dimension maps 1:1 to an existing Router attribute — the intent was lagging the block, not requesting new implementation.

title: Validation Generation: Protocol + Adapters, Two Delivery Modes
summary: How one validation intent declaration becomes runnable validation — purely the generation layer (protocol + adapters), excluding UI, component integration, the validity-model, and the conformance meta-layer. Confirms the two-mode framing is right and both modes already ship: Mode 1 (single-source generation, no runtime link) is best embodied by protovalidate (CEL on Protobuf → identical rules in Go/TS/Java/Python/C++), with JSON-Schema-as-source + per-target codegen (datamodel-code-generator, quicktype) as the pragmatic, lossier variant, and Standard Schema as the TS-only interface-consumer pattern that reduces the adapter problem from N×M to N+M; Mode 2 (backend exposes validation as a service) is best embodied by Laravel Precognition (runs the real rules without executing the controller, returns 422 + errors for live FE validation). Narrows the Mode-2 wire format to RFC 9457 application/problem+json by default with content-negotiated richer formats; models 'declare which intents you comply with' on JSON Schema's $vocabulary required/optional mechanism (consuming the #005 capability manifest); and identifies a gap to claim — RFC 9457 standardizes the error envelope but not the field-level error shape, which a WE Validation protocol could define as a first-class intent.

title: Tree-select & Hierarchical Selection
summary: Web-platform and library prior art for a hierarchical member of the droplist family, grounding backlog #064's open forks. WAI-ARIA APG and all six surveyed libraries (Ant/MUI/Fluent/Carbon/PrimeReact/Shoelace) keep three axes independent — disclosure (aria-expanded, shared with <details>/accordion), hierarchy (role=tree›treeitem›group + aria-level/setsize/posinset), and selection (aria-selected, already WE's Selection Intent) — so hierarchy earns its own intent that delegates selection/focus/type-ahead and composes a separate disclosure axis. The keyboard model is fully APG-specified (Right/Left carry expand+traversal; Up/Down never change disclosure). Any node is selectable by default (leaf-only is an opt-in constraint). Windowing is flatten-to-visible-rows so Windowed Collection needs zero tree changes; the real coupling is focus — virtual aria-activedescendant is viable because the active-always-mounted invariant already holds, with roving tabindex the alternative for giant trees and collapse-focuses-ancestor keeping focus in the projection. There is no native tree-select (optgroup can't nest). The survey also surfaced a fork the item omitted: multi-select parent/child cascade (divergent across libraries) — a configurable cascade + value-reporting dimension.

title: Menu / Menubar / Context-menu — Action Invocation
summary: Web-platform and library prior art for a Web Everything Menu block (the ARIA menu/menubar/menu-button/context-menu family — action invocation, not value selection), grounding backlog #173. Every benchmark library (Radix/Headless/MUI/Fluent/Carbon/Ant/Shoelace) models menu as a separate component from listbox/select. WAI-ARIA APG fully specifies the roles and keyboard model and — contrary to the item's premise — permits roving tabindex OR aria-activedescendant (WE chooses roving by convention, fixed). Native substrate is thin: <menu> is a <ul> alias, type=context is dead, but Popover supplies the surface and the newly-Baseline Invoker Commands API (command/commandfor + CommandEvent) is the action-dispatch substrate for the command intent (#016) — the menu's one net-new dependency. Everything else composes existing intents (Anchor, Focus Delegation roving, Type-Ahead, Selection for checkable items, Disclosure for submenus, Action for prominence). Forks: command intent vs Action (A), roving as fixed default (B), one block with a shape dimension vs three (C), checkable=Selection under a trait (D), submenu=Disclosure not the hierarchy intent (E); plus two the item omitted — separator/group semantics (F) and the menu-button double-home in the droplist roster (G).

title: Web Permissions / Notifications / Push
summary: Web-platform prior art grounding backlog #009's 'project vs intent vs fold into Feedback' call. The platform's seams force a three-way decomposition the item collapsed: the Permissions API is a cross-cutting state layer (granted | denied | prompt, spanning camera/geolocation/clipboard/notifications) — an own permission intent, not a notification render; the Notifications API is a system-level render surface (sibling to in-app Feedback and in-session Background Task) — a distinct system-notification intent; and the Push API is a server-authoritative VAPID transport/orchestration whose swappable-provider shape matches the Guard protocol — a push protocol with a CustomPushProvider lock under a narrowed project. Permissions-Policy is a deployment gate for webmanifests, not UX. Net: resolves to all three on different axes; nothing folds into Feedback.

title: Web Identity Project
summary: Web-platform and library prior art grounding backlog #012's project/protocol/intent/defer call for identity & auth. The platform already unified identity behind one browser-mediated dispatcher — navigator.credentials.get() — routing to passwords (PasswordCredential), passkeys/WebAuthn (PublicKeyCredential), federated FedCM (IdentityCredential), and Digital Credentials (mDoc) through one call and mediation enum. That is structurally a protocol with a provider/predicate seam (mirroring Guard), not a monolithic project: a webidentity project owning one credential-acquisition protocol with families as swappable CustomCredentialProviders. Authentication is back-end-authoritative — WE standardizes only the UX-only surface (mediation, credential-request declaration, auth-state predicate that feeds #178's Guard access-gate). Verbose ceremonies (SimpleWebAuthn/Hanko) are swappable adapters. Recommends authoring the thin mediation intent now, deferring the full protocol + Configurator domain. Distinct from #009 permissions.

title: Credential-Management Protocol (build design)
summary: Build-design prior art for the deferred webidentity protocol (backlog #496, blocking epic #483), after #012 settled the meta-shape (one project owning one credential protocol; families as a request dimension; identity feeds Guard). #496 ratified the protocol id as credential-management (surveyed here under the working name credential-acquisition; renamed once the member set grew past get-only to the full Credential Management API surface). Two new findings ground the build forks. (1) The credential lifecycle is four CredentialsContainer methods with asymmetric per-family support: get() is universal; create()/store() exist only for passkey + password; FedCM and Digital Credentials are get-only (enrollment is off-platform at the IdP/issuer); preventSilentAccess() is an origin-global sign-out flag. So a flat single contract forces get-only families to stub create/store — the same shape Guard solved with members. (2) The provider seam is not greenfield: every WE project owns one protocol fixing one seam resolved by a swappable CustomXProvider (Guard's CustomGuardProvider, Lifecycle's CustomLifecycleProvider, Validation, Change Tracking, Localization), native-first default → project override → custom plug; identity reuses Guard's async/server-authoritative/revocable contract. Recommends: one CustomCredentialProvider registry-resolved per family with a native-passthrough default plus a mock conformance provider for server-less demoability; lifecycle modelled as request/enrollment/session-mediation members; the Configurator domain omitted (provider + config-flavors suffice) until a real authoring need appears.

title: Scroll / Observation
summary: Web-platform prior art grounding backlog #014's 'project vs intent vs neither' call for the scroll surface. The platform ships no 'scroll domain' — just independent primitives sharing a scrollbar: IntersectionObserver (the in-view trigger), scroll-driven animations (animation-timeline — a motion concern), scroll-snap (layout), content-visibility (the virtualization floor), History.scrollRestoration (navigation), scrollend. So scroll earns neither a project nor a monolithic intent; decompose onto native seams that mostly already have homes. The one reusable residue is a thin viewport-presence mechanism intent owning the observe-vocabulary (root/rootMargin/threshold/enter-leave) that three existing IntersectionObserver consumers compose — Collection Operations' advance:auto, Prefetch's eagerness:viewport, and visibility-gated trait activation. advance:auto stays a Collection Operations dimension and composes that trigger, resolving the dated seam with one home for the trigger, three for the intent.

title: Persistence & Offline
summary: Web-platform and library prior art grounding backlog #011, which completes the data story (webresources fetches, webstates holds, nothing persists). The platform splits persistence into four orthogonal layers: durable structured storage (IndexedDB + navigator.storage.persist()), HTTP-response caching (Cache Storage + Service Worker, a webresources/fetch concern), deferred replay (Background Sync, unsupported in Safari — a JS outbox is the portable fallback), and cross-tab coordination (Web Locks). Verdict per fork: storage is a thin Protocol (CustomStorageStrategy, IndexedDB native-first, Dexie/RxDB/PouchDB adapters) — possibly a webstates facet rather than a new top-level project; optimistic UX already lives in the Loader intent, only the durable outbox is new (a fixed mechanism); conflict resolution is a strategy dimension of the existing Change Tracking protocol; offline cache leans to webresources, the durable store to webstates. Default: no new top-level project.

title: App Shell + Micro-App Compatibility Map
summary: Web-platform and industry prior art grounding backlog #104: a thin App Shell that loads independently-developed micro-apps as pages of one SPA, deciding on navigation whether the next page loads in the same SPA or needs a hard refresh, plus a Platform compatibility map. Deepened 2026-06-13: Module Federation 2.0 (mf-manifest.json + DevTools dependency graph), Native Federation, Piral, qiankun, Luigi, Bit, plus the classic declared-range resolvers (npm peerDependencies/engines, OSGi, Kubernetes version-skew). Correcting the first cut: declared manifests and rendered graphs already exist — but only for the shared-library-version dimension. The genuine novelty is a declared COEXISTENCE contract that also covers the DOM dimension (custom-element tag-names, window globals, route ownership) — which no surveyed tool declares; the ones that solve it (qiankun, Luigi) use enforced isolation, never a declared+mapped contract. Recommends: split the home — a thin app-shell-compatibility protocol owned by webmanifests plus the live map as a Plateau dashboard (Zephyr Cloud is proof the product leg is viable); declare flat (npm-peerDependencies-shaped provider ranges + OSGi-namespaced globals/tag-names/routes claims) and compute a browser-compat-data-style matrix as the rendered view (BCD is curated, not declared), with three states and an asymmetric skew window; borrow the Storage eviction model (best-effort/persistent + LRU under a mounted-app budget). Adapters-via-DI (webinjectors) shrink the incompatible set so hard refresh is the last resort.

title: Tool-agnostic Chart Config
summary: Prior-art survey grounding backlog #105: a tool-agnostic, standard JSON describing a chart (data, encodings, axes, interactions, styling intent), rendered by any conforming tool and scored on its support. Vega-Lite is the only pure-JSON, schema-published grammar of graphics (Observable Plot is a JS API; Plotly/ECharts are JSON-with-callbacks), so the recommendation is profile it rather than greenfield. The real ask is the comparison layer — the constellation's provider/registry + conformance pattern (a CustomChartRenderer registry, native-first SVG default, pluggable Vega/Plotly/ECharts adapters) like Anchor Positioning and Localization. Accessibility is first-class spec content plus a weighted conformance axis (WAI-ARIA Graphics roles, a spec-derived data-table, Vega-Lite's description channel). No webcharts project exists today, so a standalone project is net-new scaffolding — the genuine open fork.

title: Mock & Service-Proxy Dev Service
summary: Prior-art survey grounding backlog #107: a dev-time HTTP mock + service-proxy standard whose hard rule is that mocks never live in deployable code — a real local server transparently mocks/proxies APIs via config + console. Surveying MSW, Mockoon, Prism, WireMock, Pact and OpenAPI, the field splits code-defined (MSW, non-portable by design) from data-defined (the only kind that yields a portable artifact), so the schema is the standard and the server is a swappable provider — OpenAPI-anchored shape plus a WE behavior layer for latency/fault/conditional and a mock|proxy|record response-kind. Every survivor leads self-run with a hosted broker as the commercial upsell, so ship the tier-1 self-run tool first. 'One contract, two uses' (Pact/BDCT) holds only if the artifact carries consumer interactions, not just a shape — verified in webcases.

title: NL → Technical Configurator
summary: Prior-art survey grounding backlog #096: an AI front-end to the Technical Configurator where a developer describes behavior in plain language and the AI selects and wires the right strategy from the registries. The survey settles four forks. Surface: panel-first (an NL box inside the configurator that populates the requirements state exactly as a preset does), reusing the ranking, verdict badges and decision-record emitter; CLI and editor command are follow-ons. Provider contract: a surface-agnostic describe(nl, domain) → Requirements that translates intent only, leaving strategy selection to the deterministic compat engine. Grounding: a per-domain JSON schema derived from the seed run under constrained decoding, so the model cannot emit off-vocabulary axes/values. Failure: partial fill that degrades into the panel's manual mode. The registry the output is constrained to is the moat, not the model.

title: Auto-update Pipeline & Change Management
summary: Change-management prior-art survey grounding backlog #101: the orchestration that keeps an app current on deps/standards safely — trigger → security scan → cool-off → auto-branch+test → gates → merge/deploy → monitor → revert. Every tool (Dependabot, Renovate, semantic-release, changesets) splits a committed policy file from a swappable runner, so the WE shape is a thin protocol declaring update policy + capability (the escapable lock) with orchestration as a Plateau/reliability service — the same registry+provider pattern as Guard and render-strategy. The gate is graded config keyed to severity + signal, never one mechanic: a pre-merge gate (stability buffer → merge confidence → auto-merge low-risk-green, human-gate major + visual-diff) plus a post-deploy staged-rollout metric gate (auto-rollback). Migration/codemods are consumed from #102/#094 (hence blockedBy); the net-new edge is Platform-side pre-testing against consumers' suites before release.

title: Auto-update runner execution topology — build a runner vs. compose existing engines
summary: Prior-art survey + architecture prep for decision #497 (the runner that executes the already-shipped update-policy protocol from #101). Distinct ground from the #101 auto-update-pipeline survey: that covered the policy + gate model; this covers HOW the runner is built and WHERE it executes. Four findings reshape the pre-flight's 'no build home' framing. (1) The ecosystem has converged on CI-native execution, not a bespoke vendor-operated service: Dependabot consolidated its compute onto GitHub Actions (GitHub/self-hosted runners + Kubernetes ARC, GA 2025-05); Renovate ships the same runner three ways from one codebase (Mend-hosted app, self-hosted app, self-hosted GitHub Action) off one renovate.json — so the runner is a portable package + committed CI templates that run in the consumer's own CI, with a hosted convenience tier layered on, needing no new repo and mapping onto open-core-by-usage + the solo-founder self-run>service ranking. (2) Pre-merge and post-deploy execute in DIFFERENT homes by nature — pre-merge is a CI-time concern (Renovate/Dependabot), post-deploy staged rollout is a deploy-time in-cluster controller (Argo Rollouts CRD-driven metric promotion/rollback; Flagger webhook hooks) — so 'where the runner lives' was never one answer. (3) The commoditized core (trigger/scan/cool-off/test/auto-merge/metric-rollback) is off-the-shelf; the one net-new WE edge no incumbent offers is pre-testing a provider version against its CONSUMERS' suites before release via the #092 provider-consumer graph. (4) A pure compile-to-renovate.json adapter is lossy — it drops the WE-specific semantics (changelog-manifest strictest-wins severity #102, migration linkage via the upgrader engine #094, the unified pre+post gate axis, the #092 pre-test). Synthesis: a thin WE orchestrator that OWNS the update-policy gate evaluation + the #092 pre-test edge, and DRIVES incumbent engines (Renovate/Dependabot pre-merge, Argo/Flagger post-deploy) as swappable execution primitives — compose for the solved parts, own the WE-specific parts. Constellation layering ratifies by precedent (#091/#092 Fork 3): protocol→WE (shipped), reusable orchestration→FUI, operated surface→plateau-app, no-leakage held. Report: reports/2026-06-13-auto-update-runner-execution-topology.md.

title: JSX Event-Style — Function Prop vs String Behavior
summary: Framework prior art grounding backlog #051: the JSX adapter mirrors the canonical HTML adapter and accepts one event handler two ways — the function prop onclick={inc} (JSX's type-checked ergonomic draw) and the string behavior on:click="inc($event)" (the canonical form that survives to HTML). The handler-attachment surface is the most divergent across the ecosystem (React/Preact onClick={fn}, Solid on:click={fn}, Vue @click="inc($event)", Lit @click=${fn}, native inline-string vs addEventListener), so it is a spelling choice, not a semantic one. Only the string form serializes to an HTML attribute, so it must be canonical; named handlers round-trip while inline closures are one-way (lossy). Recommends authoring function-style with string canonicalization, plus a string-function display sub-toggle reusing the shipped soft html | react dialect machinery — not a new protocol.

title: Pagination: Focus & Announcement After a Page Change
summary: WAI-ARIA APG / NN-g / screen-reader-practice pass grounding backlog #059: when the user moves to a new page, where does focus land and what is announced? The load-bearing finding is that neither half is net-new vocabulary — both are already-owned intents the pagination block composes. Focus is the focus-after-view-swap question owned by Autofocus-on-Activation (landing: heading|target|preserve|auto, default heading; the APG answer), which Navigation already composes; the WebAIM thread shows no industry consensus, so it stays a configurable dimension. Announcement is the shipped clause-joined polite-status pattern (role=status + aria-atomic, W3C ARIA22 / WCAG 4.1.3) owned by Live Region Status — always announce "Page 2 of 10", a fixed mechanic, not a toggle. The platform offers no native focus/announce after a non-reload page change, so the only build is thin renderer wiring — zero new intents, zero protocols.

title: Agent File-Lock Coordination
summary: OS / database / distributed-systems prior art grounding backlog #083: a just-in-time, fine-grained file lock for when independent agents sometimes touch the same files and the file set is unknown upfront — claim right before editing, hold for seconds, release after, with a per-file FIFO queue plus crash/deadlock safeties. Each fork maps to established theory: advisory-vs-mandatory is the flock/kernel split (only a PreToolUse hook is a real lock, not a convention); crash safety is a lease + TTL + heartbeat with git as the ground-truth backstop and fencing tokens as the principled escalation; deadlock is broken via no-hold-while-blocked (the only Coffman-breaker when the lock set is unknowable); granularity stays file-level with hot shared files single-writer by policy; queue fairness is FIFO + a TTL on waiters. Meta-fork: the 2025-2026 worktree-isolation consensus already covers most value, so keep parked — build the v1 (lock.mjs + hook) only on an observed residual same-file collision.

title: Governance Persona Roster + Charter Schema
summary: Governance and access-control prior art grounding backlog #166: the platform's stakeholder personas (developer, designer, manager, translator, analyst/QA, security, legal) already ship as a charter schema rendering at plateau-app /profiles, and four model questions are unsettled. The schema exists, so the forks reshape it, not design it. RACI/RASCI decision-rights is the right vocabulary (one Accountable per gate, no system permissions) and RBAC is a category error — the personas govern who reviews/approves, not who may act. Defaults: canonical built-ins + clone-to-derive custom personas (the SaaS RBAC template guardrail); built-ins free, custom/shareable personas the paid affordance (composing #141's local-free/server-paid cost-flat rule); a shared data home both lenses read — product data, not a WE standard entity; and widen the descriptive blocksDeployment boolean to the four governance gate types (Advisory/Validating/Blocking/Escalating) so enforcement can arrive later without a schema break.

title: Dropdown Async Pagination
summary: Prior-art survey (pagination literature, the WAI-ARIA APG Feed pattern, React Aria useAsyncList, open MUI/Fluent/SAP/Radix feature-requests) grounding backlog #018's async-pagination forks for the dropdown family. The load-bearing finding: the item is largely overtaken by work landed since it was filed — cursor-vs-offset already lives in the Custom Pagination protocol, and windowing+async is a composition of Windowed Collection (dataSource:infinite) over Loader (filtering/loadingMore), which the autocomplete member already declares. So no net-new contract; the dropdown composes the three existing standards. The genuinely-open residue is three family-specific gaps: wire Collection Operations advance:manual|auto onto autocomplete (default auto + manual fallback), keep the family forward-only (load-earlier is a Feed concern), and adopt the Feed aria-setsize="-1" + aria-busy rule for the unknown-total case. No native async combobox pagination exists.

title: Web Docs as a Service
summary: Docs-as-a-service prior-art survey (Mintlify, Docusaurus, ReadMe, GitBook, Storybook+Chromatic, plus conformance/coverage dashboards) grounding backlog #091's three follow-ons before any code. The market splits ingestion two ways — docs-as-code (source of truth stays in the customer's repo) vs platform-first (vendor becomes the source of record, the lock-in WE refuses). WE is structurally docs-as-code: a customer's webmanifest+webcases are the source, so Plateau resolves and serves but never owns content (the Mintlify model). The conformance differentiator already exists on disk — reuse the /protocols/ index + capabilityMatrix + continuous-verification machinery as one pipeline, not a parallel bolt-on dashboard. Home: a managed offering under the Plateau umbrella, resolved in lockstep with Module-as-a-Service (#081), graduating to its own project only when a second consumer demands it. The open premise holds only if the webdocs generator stays self-hostable: paid = licensed hosting, not the generator.

title: Web Docs Open-Core Tiering Mechanics
summary: Pricing/metering prior-art survey (Chromatic, Mintlify, ReadMe, GitBook, Netlify, Vercel) grounding backlog #428's open-core mechanics — the metered unit, the free/paid threshold, and the billing surface — after #091 ratified the open-core-by-usage principle. The market meters on four distinct units, and the choice is the product's pricing identity: per-seat (Mintlify/GitBook — penalizes teams; Netlify is fleeing it), per-site/project (GitBook/ReadMe — simple but underprices), per-value-action (Chromatic's snapshot — meter the unit that delivers the value, graceful pause at the free ceiling), and pure infra credits (Netlify/Vercel — commoditized, captures no differentiator). Web Docs' differentiator is that the docs prove conformance, so the value-action to meter is the conformance-verified regeneration — the Chromatic-snapshot analog — reusing #089 idea-1's continuous-verification machinery as the meter. The threshold is a principle (free clears the self-host floor and onboards a real OSS/solo project; paid begins at ongoing production value), numbers deferred to the #427 build with real usage. The billing surface is a ratify, not a fork: #183's Lemon Squeezy MoR now supports usage-based/metered subscriptions, so the metered hosted model fits the chosen rail with no reopen.

title: Upgrader Version-Migration Codemods
summary: Prior-art survey (Angular ng update / migrations.json, OpenRewrite, jscodeshift/codemod) grounding backlog #191 — applying breaking-change codemods when a protocol/adapter/provider contract evolves across versions. The key finding reshapes the decision: both prerequisites #191 named already shipped. The machine-readable migration descriptor is the resolved changelog-manifest protocol (#102) — per-module entries keyed to semver severity + a migration linkage referencing the codemod that applies a breaking change, with author/integrity trust. The transform engine + provider registry + verify gate is #094's upgraderEngine (verifyUpgrade re-parses + round-trips + conformance-checks). So #191 is wiring + two design forks, not greenfield. Fork 1: the version-migration upgrader is a mode/input-adapter of the existing engine, not a distinct tool (#094's own follow-on said shared core, different input adapter). Fork 2: migration transforms are declarative-first with an imperative escape hatch — the OpenRewrite principle (declarative if it can be, imperative only when necessary), the descriptor referencing a codemod only for the complex tail. Angular's ng update supplies the version-gated, ordered, intermediate-spanning run loop; the verifyUpgrade gate is WE's moat over schematics/jscodeshift, which apply transforms without verifying conformance.

title: Development Guide & Learn Pathway
summary: Documentation-architecture prior-art survey (Diátaxis, MDN's framework-docs policy, Rails Doctrine, Vue/MDN learning-path UIs) grounding backlog #109's shape forks for an opinionated development guide surfaced as the Technical Configurator's learn pathway — deliberately neither the neutral standard (Web Everything) nor the reference impl (Frontier UI). Diátaxis maps the constellation cleanly: standard = Reference, configurator = How-to, guide = Explanation+Tutorial. Resolves to one guide surface in two registers (decision-support + philosophy/handbook); the Configurator's existing Domain type IS the single source, so the learn view is a read-only second view over the same CapabilityProvider (no drift, no second authoring path); render via webdocs (#091) from that source; free browse is the floor with a curated prev/next path as a thin ordering layer. No off-the-shelf decision-guide surface exists — it's a genuine composition of established parts.

title: Resource Directive — Async Contract
summary: Web-platform and library prior-art survey (SolidJS createResource/Suspense/ErrorBoundary, React 19 use()/useTransition, TanStack Query/Router) grounding backlog #124's four forks for the <Resource> directive — net-new with no defined behavior, currently pure spelling, unlike <For>/<Show> which the lowering compiler already round-trips. Loading and error are universally separate composable boundaries, so <Resource> owns named loading/error slots whose names ARE Loader Intent lifecycle states (resolve through the Loader Intent + Resource Loader block, no new state machine). The suspend-on-read accessor (use()/createResource) is the lowering target, lifting to a use()-style suspending read with Suspense/ErrorBoundary boundaries and a lossy diagnostic. Pagination is a Loader loadingMore + Windowed Collection composition, deferred to #018. There is no native <resource> element. Net: <Resource> is a declarative composition of the Loader Intent — no new intent, no parallel async model.

title: Dev-browser Vision
summary: Market-landscape and repo-machinery survey grounding backlog #141's four open design forks for a Chromium-based developer browser whose tooling lights up only on a standard-conformant app, depth scaling with conformance. The novel move is gating on a vendor-neutral standard rather than a framework (React/Vue/Redux DevTools are the only precedent, framework-gated). Fork 1: gate on a capability manifest and degrade per-feature (reuse the capabilityMatrix vocabulary), not all-or-nothing. Fork 2: reuse the existing verify-gated conformance-autofix loop generalized to a running app — autonomy is a configured ladder defaulting to open-PR-for-review, and live-patching a deployed app is session-only, revertible, authorization-gated. Fork 3: yes a commercial license (free panel funnel → paid browser, cost-flat open-core), deferred to first-sale. Fork 4: a distinct shell embedding plateau-app's existing configurator/profile panels, not a fork of it. The sequencing fork (extension/panel first) is already ruled.

title: Access Control — Authorization Gate
summary: Web-platform and library prior-art survey (CASL isomorphic <Can>, Angular CanActivate, React Router loaders, Vue navigation guards, OPA/PDP, LaunchDarkly entitlements, the RFC 9110 403-vs-404 existence-hiding rule) grounding backlog #178's member-design forks. The home is ruled — access control is the entry member of the Guard protocol (the entry mirror of the exit guard), built on its predicate-provider seam, not its own project. Authorization is universally factored into two surfaces fed by one ability source: a routing entry guard and a render-time element gate — so the member exposes both off one authz provider. The UX names the deny-outcome family (hide|redirect|forbid|cloak) but the forbid(403)-vs-cloak(404) existence-hiding call lives behind the provider (a security decision). Feature flags are an authority kind resolved through the same seam, not separate machinery. The trust boundary (front-end = UX mirror, back-end authoritative) is restated, not re-decided. The Navigation API is the route-guard substrate; there is no native authorization primitive.

title: Dropdown UX Behaviors
summary: Foundational best-practice survey of the full dropdown family (native <select>, custom listbox/select, filterable combobox, menu-button) across the WAI-ARIA APG, WCAG, CSS WG/MDN, and top UX authorities (NN/g, Carbon, USWDS, Baymard, React Aria, MUI). Its load-bearing finding: almost none of a dropdown's hard parts are about dropdowns — anchored positioning, dismissal-and-focus-return, focus delegation, type-ahead, the selection model, live-region status, windowed rendering, async lifecycle, validation, clearability, truncation, and grouping are general capabilities a dropdown merely composes. The survey extracted 13 cross-cutting paradigms, all materialized as intents (3 new, 5 extended, 5 pre-existing) plus the droplist composition manifest. It is the parent research of the droplist family — its one noted-but-unspecified item (async pagination beyond load-more) spun out as #018 / the Dropdown Async Pagination topic.

title: Benchmark corpus — design systems & UI libraries for competitive coverage analysis
summary: The canonical input to the competitive coverage gap-analysis program (backlog #315): an explicit, reproducible list of the leading design systems and UI libraries to benchmark, governed by stated selection criteria so a re-run lands on a comparable list. Spans five category axes — mature opinionated design systems (Material 3, Carbon, Fluent, Spectrum, Polaris, Atlassian, Primer, Lightning), headless/a11y-first primitives (Radix, React Aria, Base UI, Ark, Headless UI), web-component systems (Shoelace, Spectrum Web Components, Lion; FAST retired), popular component kits (MUI, Ant, Chakra, Mantine, shadcn/ui), and platform/standards guidance (Apple HIG, GOV.UK, WAI-ARIA APG, Open UI) — deliberately so the gap sweep is not biased toward one design philosophy. The corpus data lives in src/_data/benchmarkCorpus.json; the references.json 'Reference Design Systems' set is its curated docs-surface subset. Phase 2 (extraction, #346) and phase 3 (mapping & gap detection, #347) join against it.

title: Benchmark-corpus source currency — the FAST decommission & how the corpus retires a dead source
summary: Grounds decision #546 — the corpus source `fast` whose docsUrl went dead during #531. Research turned a 'fix the URL vs drop it' framing into a near-forced retirement plus a reusable convention. Three findings: (1) fast.design's per-component docs are genuinely gone, not moved — /docs/<1.x|2.x|3.x>/components/* 404, the historical /docs/components/<name>/ 404s, and even the path the live repo README points at (/docs/2.x/) 404s; (2) the FAST GitHub repo is NOT archived (pushed 2026-06-12, ~9.6k★) but packages/ now holds only fast-element, fast-html, fast-router, fast-build, fast-test-harness — the fast-foundation/fast-components library was REMOVED, so FAST is now a low-level web-component authoring toolkit (an element base class + templating + router), shipping none of the 96 benchmarked component/pattern/token capabilities; (3) FAST's former components were folded into Fluent UI Web Components (microsoft/fluentui) = the corpus's existing `fluent-2` source, so re-benchmarking FAST would double-count Fluent. Wayback has a complete 2024-01-28 snapshot of /docs/components/<name>/, so an archive walk is technically possible but would cite frozen URLs for a deleted component set (violates the corpus currency criterion). Under benchmarkCorpus.json's own inclusionRule, `fast` now fails docs-quality + currency-as-a-component-set and no longer occupies the web-component axis (which stays covered by shoelace/spectrum-web-components/lion). `fast` has 0 rows in benchmarkCapabilityPresence.json, so retirement breaks no presence-row reference. Recommends: retire `fast` (not repoint-to-archive, not keep-as-tooling), and mark it retired with a reason (retired/retiredDate/retiredReason) rather than delete the entry — preserving the audit trail under the #192 longitudinal-freshness model and establishing the corpus's first source-retirement convention.

title: Benchmark capability matrix — extracted components, patterns, tokens & standards
summary: Phase 2 of the competitive coverage gap-analysis program (backlog #346): the normalized capability matrix extracted from the benchmark corpus, deduped across sources into stable capability ids spanning four kinds — components, patterns, tokens, and standards. Each row carries a coarse cross-corpus ubiquity signal, standout implementations (notableIn), and a nativeAnchorHint (the native API/element it plausibly maps to) so phase 3's gap detection stays native-first. First pass keyed for diffability; exact per-source presence + deep doc URLs are deferred to a per-source fan-out. Data: src/_data/benchmarkCapabilities.json. Phase 3 (mapping & gap detection, #347) joins each row to a Web Everything entity and labels covered/partial/missing.

title: Benchmark coverage & gap detection — what Web Everything is missing
summary: Phase 3 of the competitive coverage gap-analysis program (backlog #347): each extracted capability joined to a Web Everything entity (block/intent/plug/protocol/project) and labelled covered / partial / missing / native-covered, weighed native-first. Of 96 capabilities: 52 covered, 9 native-covered, 24 partial, 11 missing. After deduping against the backlog — 9 already tracked, incl. #028 file-upload, #018 async-pagination, #064 tree-select, #010 theme-color, #022 drag-and-drop, and (found at phase-4 file time) #173/#300 menu/menubar/context-menu, #299 command-palette, #008 accordion/disclosure — 7 fileable gaps remained, ranked, led by a toast/notification block, a date/time picker block, and a unified design-token/theming system. Phase 4 (#348) filed them as candidate items #358–#364. Data: src/_data/benchmarkCoverage.json.

title: Unified design-token / theming system — how Web Everything structures the concrete-value layer
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #364 (the design-token project #010 deferred as "the technical half"). The W3C Design Tokens Community Group reached its first stable format (DTCG 2025.10, Oct 2025); the native CSS substrate (custom properties, @property typed registration, light-dark()/color-scheme, relative color syntax + color-mix) is Baseline; and the benchmark design systems (MD3 ref/sys/comp, Fluent global/alias, Spectrum, Radix 12-step scales, Apple HIG dynamic system colors) are unanimous on a 2-3 tier primitive→semantic→component taxonomy. The WE-specific reframe: WE already owns the semantic tier as Intents (surface/density/typography/motion + theme-color #010), so the token project should own the primitive + component tiers and leave the semantic tier to the intents — not coin a parallel vocabulary. Grounds four forks: home/layer, format (adopt DTCG), tier taxonomy + the #010 seam, and scheme/accent-derivation (native-auto vs curated — the one divergent call). Report: reports/2026-06-12-design-token-theming-system.md.

title: Provider↔consumer relationship graph & governance — ingestion, seam-contracts, and the WE/Plateau split
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #092 (the control plane that makes Plateau an enterprise web platform manager). Two ingestion models are mature industry patterns with a documented complementary consensus: build-time/declared (Backstage catalog-info.yaml spec.providesApis/consumesApis; CycloneDX SBOM) — complete and auditable but lost-at-runtime and hand-authored-drift; and runtime-agent (OpenTelemetry service-graph connector) — only-accurate-from-real-traffic but stale-fast. The field recommends both: static discovery validated by runtime, edges marked potential until trace-confirmed. The WE-specific reframe: WE introspectable registries/injectors/contexts/events ARE the graph, so the build-time manifest is auto-derived (no hand-authored catalog-info.yaml bottleneck) — making build-first staging natural. Seam-contracts map to consumer-driven contract testing (Pact broker as the safe-to-deploy-together source of truth; drift detection reactive to proactive), reused from WE protocol conformance contracts recorded bidirectionally. Home splits per the constellation: graph-model and contracts are the WE open standard, aggregation and UI and governance are the Plateau licensed product. Report: reports/2026-06-12-provider-consumer-graph.md.

title: Business-rule manager with proof of compliance — rule meta-schema, proof/trust model, enforcement seam
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #093 (a Plateau offering to author/version/govern/enforce business rules declaratively and emit an auditable proof they were applied). Two open standards answer different forks: DMN (OMG Decision Model and Notation) is the business-friendly rule meta-schema (decision tables + FEEL expression language) for authoring; OPA/Rego is the policy-as-code enforcement architecture (Policy Decision Point evaluates, Policy Enforcement Point enforces, decision logs record every verdict and why, replayable). Separate-and-decouple plus the standardize-the-meta-schema doctrine point at a new project adopting DMN rather than extending webvalidation (input invalidity is a different concern from domain policy). Proof-of-compliance is a known tamper-evidence ladder: hash chain (SHA-256 each record links the previous) to Merkle tree (O(log n) inclusion proofs) to transparency log (anchor the root externally), with blockchain as the heavy overkill end; the proof content is OPA-style decision logs anchored to webtraces spans, framed as OSCAL-aligned machine-readable compliance artifacts. Enforcement is the OPA PDP/PEP split observed at a trace point, runtime as the baseline proof-emitting venue. The divergent call is how far up the tamper-evidence ladder to go. Report: reports/2026-06-12-business-rule-proof-of-compliance.md.

title: Expressive symbols & reactions — emoji rendering, picker surface, sticker, reaction interaction
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #370 (a web-platform standard for emoji / sticker / reaction — genuine whitespace beside the icon intent, which covers UI iconography only). The survey (web platform standards + the benchmark design systems) says decompose into SEPARATE standards rather than one family, matching the only system that ships all of this first-class: Atlassian splits @atlaskit/emoji (glyph + EmojiPicker) from @atlaskit/reactions, where reactions imports the emoji provider but is never merged in. Four paradigms: (1) expressive-glyph rendering — a new intent sibling to icon (meaningful role=img+aria-label vs decorative aria-hidden; Unicode variation selectors VS15/VS16 + emoji modifier base / skin tone + ZWJ sequences; native-font-vs-image-set render strategy); (2) picker surface — composed, not a new primitive: a searchable popover grid over the existing droplist/autocomplete blocks specifying the browse-grid to search-listbox transform; (3) sticker — folded into glyph rendering as a custom-image-glyph mode (only Apple HIG models it first-class); (4) reaction interaction — a block + intent that consumes glyph rendering: aria-pressed toggle + aggregate count + who-reacted roster + optimistic update (Atlassian ReactionSummary contract baseline), set policy defaulting to a constrained quick-set with an allowAllEmojis escape hatch (most-shipped and most-permissive; GitHub's locked-8 is the rigid extreme), count changes announced via live-region-status. Nothing here is a protocol — no swappable-vendor interop story. Report: reports/2026-06-12-expressive-symbols-reactions.md.

title: Design-reference corpus taxonomy — how to seed categories, registers & first-run scope
summary: The taxonomy seed for the design-reference screenshot corpus (backlog #382/#394): how to classify and grow the worklist of real web-app screenshots so design patterns can be codified later. Surveys how the established reference galleries taxonomise UIs — Mobbin’s faceted model (app-category × UI-pattern/flow × UI-element × platform × design-trend), Page Flows / Screenlane’s screen-type axis (onboarding, dashboard, settings, checkout, empty/error…) — and the information-architecture finding that a faceted controlled vocabulary that grows beats both a closed enum (too rigid) and free-text folksonomy (synonym/polysemy noise), the way G2/Capterra keep evolving keyed category taxonomies. Three implications drive the seed: split the conflated ‘designRegister’ into productRegister (archetype, deterministic) + visualStyle (named aesthetic — flat/material/glass/neobrutalist — deferred to the vision pass); store the seed as an open-growing keyed registry (design-refs/taxonomy.json, mirroring benchmarkCorpus.json); and scope the first run by scarcity-weighted grow-targets that fill the registers thin today (everything outside dev-tools/editors). Internal research-corpus tooling — no standard, zero project-facing lock-in.

title: Deployed-app live-patch safety — isolation, authorization, revertibility, audit
summary: Safety design for the dev browser’s riskiest capability (backlog #410, carved from the dev-browser vision #141 Fork 2): an AI applying a fix to a *running deployed* app. Surveys established practice across the four concerns #141 named. The central finding reframes the capability: Chrome DevTools ‘Local Overrides’ is an exact precedent for a session-scoped, server-untouched patch — modified files served only in the viewer’s own browser session, active only while it runs — so the dev browser can patch the live *view* of a deployed app (the demo value) without ever mutating the deployed artifact; the PR stays the only path to a real change. The heavy alternatives (server-side canary; kernel-style live-fleet hot-patch via kpatch/Ksplice, where kpatch can’t even revert without a reboot) are contained or rejected. Authorization follows break-glass + just-in-time privileged access (per-patch, expiring, alerting, optional two-party); lifetime is session-scoped with a hard TTL + kill switch; audit reuses the app’s own introspectable trace substrate with SOC2 minimum fields. A product capability, not a standard — and the one case where the usual most-permissive default is inverted to most-restrictive-safe.

title: Research-freshness model — history, metadata home, staleness, review horizon
summary: Prior-art survey for the metadata + versioning model that keeps /research/ topics alive over time (backlog #441, foundation forks of epic #192). No design exists yet — none of lastReviewed / supersedes / reviewHorizon are in the tree. Surveys four orthogonal axes. History/supersedes: RFC (Obsoletes/Obsoleted-by), PEP (Replaces/Superseded-By + Status: Superseded), MADR and W3C TR all converge on immutable dated records + bidirectional supersedes pointers + a superseded status value, never an in-place overwrite — matching /research/'s existing dated reports/YYYY-MM-DD-{slug}.md snapshots, which only lack the pointer+status. Metadata home: per-doc frontmatter is the prevailing source of truth (Microsoft ms.date, Hugo lastmod), but because WE's report is an immutable snapshot and the topic is the living unit, the topic-level review date belongs on the registry (also the render + validator source) — and last-changed (dateModified) must stay distinct from last-reviewed, which ms.date wrongly conflates. Staleness: the norm is a reader badge + a maintainer warning, never a hard build failure (Vale fails only on error severity) — confirming badge+warning coexist by design rather than being an either/or. Review horizon (the axis the item omitted): per-document reviewBy with volatility tiers beats a single global interval, modeled on RFC 5861's freshness-lifetime + stale-while-revalidate grace window rather than a hard cliff — the one genuinely divergent call.

title: Collection-operations coordinator — home & shape (block vs composition vs headless instance)
summary: Prep prior-art survey for decision #452 (unblocks build #369; surfaced by app A #317). Builds on the settled collection-operations intent: data-table already owns filter/sort/group (applyPipeline) and pagination owns page (PageState), but nothing owns the WHOLE filter→sort→group→page pipeline over one collection, so data-table's click-to-sort sorts only the current page (#369's bug). The item framed one fork — standalone block vs documented composition — but the survey reshapes it into two axes. Home: TanStack Table (row-model pipeline ending in a pagination row model), AG Grid (RowModel + a PaginationProxy slicing the tail), and MUI X DataGrid (internal pagination after filter/sort) all run page LAST over the whole set and all own coordination in ONE place; nobody ships docs-only — confirming a standalone primitive over documented composition.
