Shape (the reshaping finding): the dominant home is a headless INSTANCE (TanStack 'table instance', AG Grid 'row model') that owns the pipeline and delegates rendering — neither a rendered block (MUI's fused monolith is the opposite of WE's compose-don't-merge) nor docs-only. That maps onto WE's existing DataTableBehavior/PaginationBehavior convention, so the coordinator is a headless CollectionOperationsBehavior + optional <collection-operations> element wrapper, no markup of its own. Page stage stays owned by pagination's PageState (compose, never merge); client-vs-server execution is UX-only (Technical Configurator), not a fork. No net-new vocabulary.

title: Background Task reload-durability — durable execution vs durable state (scope & guard)
summary: Prep platform-substrate survey for decision #450 (unblocks the durability:reload tier build #134). The Background Task Surface ships a route-only baseline (navigationGuard arms a beforeunload + Navigation-API confirm); durability:reload is the opt-in tier that promises work surviving a full reload. Two coupled forks gate it: (1) scope beyond fetches, (2) does durability:reload relax navigationGuard:warn. The survey settles fork 1 by reshaping it: there is NO native API that runs arbitrary client-side computation in the background across a reload — Background Fetch continues only predetermined transfers while page+SW are closed (Chromium-only); service workers are event-driven and killed at ~30s idle / 5min per event; Background/Periodic Sync are best-effort network-defer; Web Locks die with their context. The only non-fetch option is checkpoint→IndexedDB→resume-on-return (classic checkpoint-restart), which is a categorically WEAKER, different guarantee: durable EXECUTION (work continues while away, Background Fetch) vs durable STATE (work pauses; only a save-point survives; the user resumes on return). Conflating both under durability:reload would overclaim. Recommendation: ship durability:reload as transfer-backed durable execution now; non-fetch compute becomes a distinct future durability:resumable (checkpointed) value, not a rival definition. Fork 2: relax navigationGuard:warn by default when durability:reload is armed, and re-arm the guard on degradation to route-only (work no longer survives). No invented jargon; durable-execution/durable-state framing + checkpoint/state-rehydration vocabulary.

title: MaaS hosted-tier economics — build-vs-ride and the freemium pattern
summary: Prep economics survey for product-decision #451 (gates phase d of MaaS distribution #087; open-core cluster #089/#091/#097). The item framed one fork — a hosted CDN-like Module-as-a-Service free for public components / paid for private / both. The survey reshapes it from free-vs-paid into BUILD-vs-RIDE. The 'free public ESM CDN for any npm package' function is already a commoditized free service: esm.sh and jsDelivr serve any published package as ESM, globally, free, with bandwidth DONATED by Cloudflare/Fastly (jsDelivr has no paid tier; its founder personally subsidizes origin). Building a free public tier rebuilds a commodity and inherits its maintenance liability — Skypack is the cautionary tale (a Cloudflare/GCP/AWS-backed smart-ESM-CDN by the Snowpack team that went unmaintained since ~2023, breaks on modern JS, recurring outages, users abandoned without notice). 'Free public + paid private' is the universal registry pattern (npm Pro $7/mo, GitHub Packages free-public + metered private $0.25/GB storage $0.50/GB egress; JSR free-only/Deno-subsidized) but the free half only works because someone else pays the bandwidth. Component-hosting businesses that survive charge for a workflow value-add not bytes (Chromatic = hosted visual testing $149/mo+, free for OSS; Bit pivoted away from component distribution to AI dev workspaces). A global 24/7 public CDN is operationally untenable for a solo operator (uncapped egress, abuse, forever maintenance liability) — squaring with the project's defer-live-serve monetization stance. Recommendation: ride existing CDNs for the public tier (publish components to npm; esm.sh/jsDelivr serve them); reserve build effort for a thin paid value-add layer needing no CDN infra (private/curated registry metered like GitHub Packages, provenance/SRI+SLA, usage analytics off jsDelivr's stats API, migration/conformance tooling) — the Chromatic playbook, open-core, deferred to plateau-app per #091. Product call: recommendation grounds it, the product owner ratifies.

title: Polyglot MaaS origin — codegen vs Wasm, source-of-truth & cross-language conformance
summary: Prep prior-art survey for decision #463 (blocked by #461, the canonical JS Fetch-standard origin = reference impl; parent MaaS #081). The self-hosted MaaS origin is today only a JS Fetch handler; enterprises run .NET/Java, so a JS-only origin forces a JS sidecar. #463 asks how WE projects a native origin outward via a forward/codegen adapter (the inverse of WE's ingest adapters). Three forks: (a) mechanism, (b) source of truth, (c) conformance. The survey reshapes fork (a) from three options to four. Mechanism: AWS Smithy proves the IDL→multi-language-server-codegen pattern (generates routing/serde/validation/types, leaves a business-logic seam, protocol-agnostic) but server-codegen maturity is lumpy (no off-the-shelf .NET/Go server emitter; Java GA 2026 but client-leaning); OpenAPI Generator reaches many languages and is HTTP-shaped but quality is per-generator. The FOURTH option is compile-once-to-Wasm + host bindings (WebAssembly Component Model + WIT): one binary embedded by each host (.NET componentize-dotnet/Wasmtime, JVM chicory, Go wazero) structurally eliminates cross-language drift — serious in 2026 (Wasm 3.0 ratified Sept 2025, WASI P2 stable, server-side now majority prod use) but host SDKs are preview-grade. Source of truth: every precedent authors a language-neutral contract and treats each language including the original as an output — favor the protocols.json#maas-versioning protocol (projectable to OpenAPI for the HTTP-GET shape) as authority, JS as just another target, #461 as reference impl not SoT. Conformance: a shared spec does NOT guarantee interop — only a conformance suite does (protobuf: same spec, 0 vs 1847 failures across libs; Connect/gRPC suite found 22+ bugs in Google's own gRPC v1.0); adopt golden-vectors + reference-impl + runner gating each release — the single most load-bearing decision, orthogonal to the codegen-vs-Wasm choice. Recommendation: generation-adapter codegen from a neutral SoT with a deterministic-core/HTTP-shell split, gated by a conformance suite, Wasm named as the convergence target for the core once host SDKs mature.

title: Responsive / container-query layout — placement against the existing breakpoint & layout intents
summary: Prep prior-art survey for decision #467 (parent: book-triage epic #111). The book candidate 'constraint-based / container-query responsive layout' (FlexRow-style flow, ResizeObserver, container vs media queries) reads as homeless, but the survey reshapes the fork: the concern is already substantially modeled by two existing intents — `breakpoint` (draft: adaptation strategy mobile-first/desktop-first/strict-range + semantic steps compact/medium/expanded, explicitly 'mapped to concrete media/container queries') and `layout` (concept: app-shell regions — drawer/rail/pane/dock, push/overlay/rail). So the original 'new project vs intent vs webpositioning' framing collapses: NOT a new project (no provider seam, interchange schema, or vendor-interop contract to standardize — the same 'not every gap is a project' discipline that ruled #409 master-detail to a plain intent), and NOT webpositioning (that project anchors floating elements to a reference — an orthogonal concern). Web-platform grounding (2026): CSS container size queries are Baseline since 2023 (Chrome/FF/Safari) with `container-type: inline-size` and `cqi`/`cqw` units (1% of container inline size); `@container` mirrors `@media` syntax but resolves against an element's container rather than the viewport; style queries are Chrome-family only and scroll-state queries landed Chrome Dec 2025; ResizeObserver is the imperative substrate for cases CSS can't express. Industry: Material 3 moved AWAY from rigid pixel breakpoints to semantic Window Size Classes (compact <600 / medium 600-839 / expanded 840-1199 / large / extra-large) + Canonical Layouts with repositioning — which is exactly the vocabulary the breakpoint intent already borrows. Three forks survive: (1) PLACEMENT — fold into the existing breakpoint + layout intents (extend, don't create) [default], over a new intent or project; (2) SCOPE — one `breakpoint` intent with an added `scope: viewport | container` dimension [default] vs two separate intents, since the semantic-steps model and `change` event are identical and only the reference frame differs (textbook expose-the-axis-as-a-dimension, not a split); (3) FLEXROW — a small deferred webblocks block (intrinsic auto-flow container realizing breakpoint at container scope, ResizeObserver as impl substrate, CSS @container/flex-wrap as the native-first default) [default] vs a layout-intent dimension (rejected: layout is app-shell altitude, FlexRow is content-flow) vs nothing (rejected: the semantic vocabulary + container-query wiring is the standard-worthy part, not the flex CSS). Recommendation carried into #467: no new project, no new intent — extend breakpoint with a scope dimension, leave layout as-is, and defer a FlexRow webblocks block.

title: Portable Cross-Field Rule Expression — CEL vs shape-only for Mode-1 validation generation
summary: Prep prior-art survey for decision #465 (webvalidation, under epic #085). The shipped v1 validation-generation vocabulary is shape-only — 13 closed intents (required/type/min/max/pattern/format/step/min-items/max-items/enum/custom) in validation-generation/provider.ts:39-58 — each statically emittable to every Mode-1 target (HTML, Zod, Pydantic, JSON Schema). Cross-field and conditional logic ('endDate after startDate', 'require X when Y=z') was deferred because the Mode-1 fidelities diverge: protovalidate carries portable CEL rules, JSON-Schema-codegen is shape-only, Standard Schema is interface-only. The fork: define/adopt a portable rule-expression layer so cross-field emits statically to all Mode-1 targets, or stay shape-only and treat cross-field as Mode-2-only (server-authoritative, already settled at RFC 9457 problem+json via Laravel-Precognition-style evaluation in the 2026-06-06 report). Survey findings: (1) CEL (Google Common Expression Language) is the proven multi-language portable rule layer — non-Turing-complete, linear-time, cross-field via '&&' (this.min<=this.max); official runtimes in Go/C++/Java/Python (cel-expr-python open-sourced Mar 2026) plus community JS/TS evaluators (cel-js, @marcbachmann/cel-js zero-dep + tree-shakeable). protovalidate proves it at v1.0 scale across 5 languages with NO codegen. (2) JSONLogic is a lighter JSON-AST alternative with broad language coverage but weaker typing/ecosystem and verbose syntax. (3) JSON-Schema conditionals (if/then/else, dependentRequired) survive shape-schema codegen but can't express value-comparison cross-field ('endDate > startDate') — dependentRequired only covers property-presence, and nested if/then/else under allOf gets 'very messy'. (4) CUE is config-altitude, not a per-field rule fit. Architectural crux: adopting a portable layer means CustomValidationAdapter (provider.ts:135-147) must carry an expression compiler — emit() (provider.ts:146) would translate a neutral rule into each target's idiom — whereas today the contract is shape-only and the only predicate escape hatch is the non-portable 'custom' intent (provider.ts:57). cross-field/conditional already exist as OPTIONAL capability features in the manifest (capability-manifest/provider.ts:48-50, 'absence is reportable, never a silent no-op') so the declared-compliance + flag-lossy seam is already in place. Recommendation carried into #465: keep the v1 adapter contract shape-only-required with cross-field an opt-in adapter capability; Mode-2 is the default authoritative home for cross-field now; a portable Mode-1 cross-field layer is a deferred opt-in that, IF/when built, ADOPTS CEL (reuse the proven external interop language — never mint a WE rule format, which would be lock-in for no interop gain) rather than inventing an AST or settling for JSON-Schema-conditionals-only. The transpile-to-native vs embed-a-runtime choice is an adapter implementation detail (representation-neutral contract), not a standards fork.

title: Web Docs self-host primitive form — rendering contract, islands, FUI authoring path
summary: Form grounding for backlog #425 (the FUI self-host Web Docs primitives, under the #091/#398 Web Docs product line). #091/#398 settled the home (FUI ships the primitives) and the what, not the form. Reading the tree corrects the stale 'block vs JSX vs package' framing: @frontierui/jsx-runtime is an HTML-mirror JSX factory that builds light DOM and IS FUI's content path — but it has no server render-to-string anywhere, while a docs product's load-bearing need is static HTML (host-anywhere, SEO, no-JS readable). So the real axis is the RENDERING CONTRACT. Prior art names the answer: islands architecture (server-render static HTML by default, hydrate only interactive islands — Astro/Fresh/Qwik; docs sites are its canonical zero-JS use case), with Declarative Shadow DOM as the native server-render mechanism for custom elements. FUI's behavior blocks (droplist/tabs/navigation/for-each) are already light-DOM islands by construction, and jsx-runtime builds light DOM, so the static skeleton needs NO DSD. Recommendation: author primitives as JSX on jsx-runtime + a new server string-emit (the only real gap — carve as a separable FUI-core prerequisite build), compose existing behavior blocks as light-DOM islands (hydrate-optional, degrades to static HTML), packaged as a single published @frontierui/webdocs-ui. Rejected: client-runtime SPA (weak self-host floor) and a Nunjucks/server-template form (second paradigm + engine lock-in). One open judgment call: hydrate-optional vs pure-static for interactive panels — defaulted to hydrate-optional, confidence medium.

title: Source-Awareness Substrate & IDE Bridge
summary: Prior-art survey grounding backlog #562 — how a dev browser maps a rendered DOM node back to the source construct that produced it, on a DEPLOYED (possibly minified, source-maps-stripped) app, then acts on the repo (jump to file:line, open a PR, drive the IDE). The central finding: the substrate is two orthogonal provider chains, not one mechanism — a node→source RESOLVER chain and an IDE-BRIDGE chain — and any resolver composes with any bridge. Resolver precedence: build-emitted string-literal data-* source anchor (Sentry's @sentry/babel-plugin-component-annotate is the live precedent — the ONLY family that survives a stripped prod build; least-leaky variant is an opaque-id + sidecar manifest, a design sweet spot no shipped product occupies) → framework debug metadata (__source/_debugSource, LocatorJS, click-to-react-component — dev-only, and React 19 removed _debugSource) → source maps (ECMA-426; JS-position granularity, usually stripped). Bridge precedence: VS Code extension with a localhost server (true two-way: WorkspaceEdit patches + active-project enumeration, needs install) → File System Access API (zero-install/zero-server deployed-tab patch-write, Chromium-only) → vscode://file deep link (jump-only, universal) → dev-server launch-editor (localhost dev only). Cold-deployed degrades to inert without a build-emitted anchor — so the anchor is the keystone. WE reframe: WE apps are already introspectable, so the anchor is a self-description extension contract the app emits and the dev-browser consumes (no-leakage seam) — standard owns the contract, Plateau dev-browser owns the resolver/bridge.

title: Bot-PR mechanics — how the fix-loop opens a pull request
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #578 (Cluster A of #562) — the VCS-interaction surface the #141 fix-loop's last step (report → propose → verify → OPEN A PR) assumes. #562 surfaced eight axes; this survey collapses them to ONE forced invariant + TWO genuine on-merit forks, the rest support-all-coherent (same shape #562 used for its provider registries). Invariant (ratify, not weigh): generated code is NOT privileged — a generated fix runs the identical review + code-compliance gates as a human PR (lint, tests, type-check, static analysis/quality gate, branch protection, required review), no bypass; the autofix verify gate (scripts/autofix/engine.mjs:229-247) is necessary-but-not-sufficient; auto-merge reachable only after every gate is green (the #141/#410 autonomy ladder). 2025 agent-PR consensus (GitHub Copilot coding agent runs the same branch-protection + required-checks gates; separate analysis from execution with a human approval gate) confirms it as a ratify. Fork 1 — forge auth, bot identity & commit signing: GitHub Apps beat PATs for unattended automation (short-lived ~1h installation tokens, fine-grained perms, org-owned, built-in bot identity, API commits auto-signed), but a browser/extension can't safely hold an App private key, so the genuine either/or is the CREDENTIAL SOURCE: (A) reuse the IDE-bridge's existing git auth [default v1 — the #576/#577 bridge is already authenticated, zero new credential surface, user identity] · (B) a GitHub-App backend broker [hosted/org-bot tier — Plateau backend mints short-lived tokens, bot identity, auto-signed] · (C) user OAuth/PAT in the browser [rejected as primary — long-lived credential exposure]. Identity+signing RIDE the auth choice (not a separate fork); couples to #410's authorization dial. Fork 2 — PR evidence payload: mint a STANDARD-OWNED conformance-evidence manifest (emitted by the app's self-description/trace substrate, reusing capabilityMatrix.json:1 vocabulary — the same app-emits/tool-consumes shape as #575's anchor contract; Plateau renders it into the PR; runtime-agnostic so polyglot fix-loops emit the same manifest = forward-adapter reach; a CONTRACT not a Protocol — no swappable-vendor interop) [recommended] vs freeform Plateau PR markdown only [no contract, re-invented per impl]; composes with (not duplicates) #410 Fork 4-A's audit record. Support-all-coherent (NOT decisions): forge-agnosticism = a forge provider registry (open-PR abstracted per forge — GitHub/GitLab/Gitea/Forgejo/Bitbucket; every provider used, precedence+degradation only — exactly #562's bridge-registry ruling; prior art: Renovate Platform interface lib/modules/platform/types.ts, Woodpecker forge.Forge, git-pkgs/forge, go-git-providers; v1 ships GitHub first = prioritization not a fork); git-flow/PR-strategy policy dials (draft-vs-ready, target branch, one-per-PR vs batched, rebase/squash/merge, auto-merge — defaults live in the compliance layer #579, read not invented by the bot); conflict/concurrency (rebase-on-stale, detect-and-skip racing fixes), revert/rollback (normal revert PR via the same gates, autonomy-gated per #410), monorepo/multi-repo targeting (derived from the resolver — repo is wherever the resolved file:line lives; cross-repo → independent PR per repo, atomic cross-repo merge rejected).

title: MaaS execution-conformance gate policy — fold-into-default vs optional-toolchain suite
summary: Prep prior-art survey for decision #549 (slice 3 of epic #507; ratified parent #463 fork a/c). #506 ships a deterministic, language-neutral conformance runner (runner.ts) that drives any origin — a ConformanceTarget = (vector) => Promise<ActualResponse> — against frozen golden vectors and asserts byte-identical fidelity. #548 added the first FOREIGN target: a byte-locked C# origin (GeneratedMaaSOrigin.cs), drift-gated today only by a string-match snapshot, not by compiling + running it. #549 closes that gap: wire the C# origin as a live subprocess ConformanceTarget (dotnet-hosted) so divergence in EXECUTED behaviour is caught. The transport is already settled in code (runner.ts pre-commits a subprocess target reading the same golden.json); the only open dimension is GATE POLICY — running the C# target needs a dotnet toolchain (compile + execute), heavy and not universally present. Fork: (A) fold into the default gate — every dev/CI run compiles + drives the C# target (max drift safety, hard dotnet dependency everywhere, slow), or (B) a separate opt-in check:maas-conformance suite that detects dotnet and skips-with-notice otherwise (portable/fast default; drift can land if unrun). Prior art is unambiguous: every mature cross-language conformance suite (Protobuf conformance over a sub-process pipe; connectrpc/conformance = a standalone connectconformance binary driving the target over stdin/stdout, integrated as a SEPARATE CI workflow not each library’s default unit suite; OpenXR CTS; IPFS gateway-conformance) runs the foreign target as a separate, toolchain-gated job — never folded into the host library’s fast unit gate. Local precedent agrees: test:integration (Playwright, heavy toolchain) is already a separate script from test:unit (vitest run), and check:standards doesn’t run vitest at all. dotnet exists locally at /usr/local/share/dotnet/dotnet but is NOT on PATH, so even locally a default-gate fold needs explicit toolchain discovery. Recommendation: default to the opt-in suite (B) — matches the canonical protobuf/Connect pattern and the repo Playwright precedent, keeps the inner loop portable; keep A available as a CI posture layered on B (promote the suite into a required gate where the toolchain is guaranteed), not a separate impl; the free byte-level source snapshot stays in the default vitest run regardless.

title: Persona as a First-Class Preset Primitive
summary: Prior-art grounding backlog #564: is persona a single first-class primitive of which the governance roster (#166) and the agile-role operating model (#563) are both projections? A persona is a named preset over composable concepts (preferences + surfaces-lit-up), not a closed RBAC group. VS Code Profiles is the mainstream preset-over-composables pattern (clone-to-derive, temporary, shareable); CrewAI role/goal/backstory agent personas are the agile-role lens, already named in industry — but both are patterns, not a shared schema. Even Persona-Based Access Control (PBAC) keeps a persona a lens mapped onto permissions, never authorization itself. The on-disk Profile (schema.ts:101) is a governance charter with no preferences/surfaces field, so unifying would be a costly refactor against a second lens that has no runtime. Defaults: name the shared pattern not a unified schema (bias toward separation); lens-only forever (access stays #178); name the role-switching primitive as a #563 methodology concept.

title: Registry Generic Type-Architecture: Class-`Key` vs Method-`get<Key>`
summary: Prior-art grounding backlog #582: the FU and WE plugs trees shipped mutually-incompatible public type contracts on three core registry surfaces (CustomContext, CustomTextNodeRegistry, CustomElementRegistry); #580 converges them into one shared runtime, which can carry only one form. The real divergence is in CustomContext.ts (the base class implementing Registry) — CustomContextRegistry.ts is byte-identical between repos. WE puts a class-level Key type param on CustomContext (defaulted to keyof ContextValue, and never overridden by any consumer in the workspace — it is dead) and mistypes the Registry Definition slot as the whole record, omitting values()/entries() and returning void from delete(); FU uses a single ContextValue param with a method-level get<Key extends keyof ContextValue>, conforms to Registry<value, string> correctly, and implements the full Map-shaped surface (values/entries/delete:boolean). The platform Map<K,V> + iterator protocol is the vocabulary the codebase's own Registry interface mirrors. Default A: adopt FU's method-level-generic string-keyed Map-shaped form — native-first, the only Registry-conformant side, and zero-consumer-cost. Riders: adopt FU's typings (ImplementedTextNode<any>; concrete upgrade(root:Node); options? optional) and port WE's ensureNativelyConstructible runtime up as additive (not a fork).

title: Mention / trigger-character inline picker — the @/:/#// surface that opens a picker mid-text
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #591 (whitespace surfaced by #370 Fork 4): WE has input/autocomplete/type-ahead (inputs + whole-field search-listbox) but no mention / trigger-character pattern — the surface that watches an editable region, detects a boundary-anchored trigger char (@/:/#//), extracts the query, anchors a popover to the CARET, routes results through a listbox, and on selection inserts back into the text (the in-editor :-emoji picker #592 is one instance). Two genuine forks; the rest collapses to support-all. Home & shape → a new BLOCK + INTENT (not a mode of autocomplete, not intent-only): every surveyed system ships the trigger-detection engine as runnable code (ProseMirror/Tiptap Suggestion utility — char/allowSpaces/startOfLine/allowedPrefixes/minQueryLength; GitHub <text-expander> keys + text-expander-change/value events; Lexical useBasicTypeaheadTriggerMatch minLength/maxLength), composing the general picker surface #588 for results. Scope (the divergent fork) → DETECTION-ONLY / insertion-agnostic: emit the resolved selection {key, query, range, item} and let the consumer commit it — the GitHub <text-expander> model — vs ProseMirror's Suggestion.command which couples insertion into the editor doc. Detection-only decouples it from any document model, so it works in input/textarea/contenteditable uniformly and composes the rich-text standard #590 for atom/token insertion WITHOUT being blocked on it. Everything else is supported by default: open trigger-set config registry (@/:/#// are conventions, project extends); boundary/query dimensions with most-permissive defaults; caret anchoring composes the anchor intent via a virtual element (virtual-elements topic; Range.getBoundingClientRect in contenteditable, mirror-div in input/textarea). Invariants: virtual focus (aria-activedescendant, DOM focus stays in text — APG editable-combobox-with-list-autocomplete; roving tabindex is broken), suspend detection during IME composition, not a protocol (no vendor-swap interop). No native HTML primitive (datalist is whole-field; combobox role is the closest vocabulary). Report: reports/2026-06-14-inline-trigger-mention-pattern.md.

title: Cross-cutting accessible-name intent — one naming policy across glyphs, icons, media, images
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #596 (surfaced by #370 Fork 3): WE has one a11y intent — live-region-status — but it owns ANNOUNCEMENT (aria-live), not NAMING; there is no accessible-name intent (no reusable role=img/aria-label/aria-labelledby policy). Should naming be a CROSS-CUTTING intent composed across icon/expressive-symbol/media/image/avatar, or stay a PER-INTENT dimension each owns itself (the way #587 currently owns its accessible-name axis and validation owns aria-invalid/aria-describedby)? Two findings reshape it. (1) Name COMPUTATION is already a platform standard: W3C AccName 1.2 defines a fixed precedence aria-labelledby > aria-label > native (img alt/label/text) > title; the accessible name is read-only computed state — you provide inputs, the browser computes — so there is NO swappable-vendor runtime behavior and the concern is NOT a protocol (re-minting AccName = lock-in for zero interop gain; native-first). (2) The only cross-cutting naming primitive in the prior art is a thin wrapper — Radix AccessibleIcon (label + visually-hidden text + aria-hidden on the glyph); Material 3 / Carbon / Fluent use per-component label props. So the concern genuinely recurs AND its field solution is a thin policy, not a mechanism. Recommendation: MINT a cross-cutting accessible-name intent (Fork 1, med-high — separate-and-decouple: the policy recurs unchanged across icon/glyph/media/image/avatar) that OWNS only the declarative policy axes — naming (meaningful|decorative) + labelSource (explicit|referenced|derived-fallback, never empty: CLDR short name for emoji, alt for images, never raw codepoint/filename; sticker/custom-image has no fallback so label is required) — and DEFERS name computation to the platform AccName algorithm (Fork 2, high). Most-permissive default: meaningful + derived fallback, decorative is opt-in (matches #587). Everything else is support-all: orthogonal to live-region-status (naming != announcement); #587 keeps its interim dimension and graduates to COMPOSING this intent once minted (migration note, not a blocker); WCAG 2.5.3 Label-in-Name constrains the policy. Report: reports/2026-06-14-cross-cutting-accessible-name-intent.md.

title: Rich-text / contenteditable editing — decompose the editor; the document model is the only Protocol
summary: Prior-art survey grounding decision #590 (whitespace surfaced by #370 Fork 4): WE has no rich-text/contenteditable/WYSIWYG editing standard — only the input intent and type-ahead cover single-line text + search. The load-bearing finding: the web ships NO monolithic editor — it ships orthogonal primitives (the editing SURFACE: contenteditable Baseline 2015 + EditContext Chromium-only 2024; the OPERATION VOCABULARY: InputEvent.inputType formatBold/insertOrderedList/… verbatim, with execCommand deprecated; SANITIZATION: HTML Sanitizer setHTML, emerging; DECORATIONS: CSS Custom Highlight API) and leaves the DOCUMENT MODEL to incompatible vendor engines (ProseMirror schema · Lexical nodes · Slate tree · Quill Delta/Parchment · ADF · roosterjs). Every serious library is layered (headless engine vs UI); no general design system (MD3/Carbon/Fluent) ships a first-class editor at all. So WE standardizes rich-text editing as SEPARATE composable standards along a substrate→engine→formatting→serialization seam (separation bias; the #370 provider→consumer precedent) — NOT one component. Layer map: editing surface → Capability (contenteditable floor + EditContext capability-upgrade, auto-negotiated like webrealtime transport); document model → Protocol + adapters (CustomEditorEngine contract + registry, native-first contenteditable+InputEvent default, library adapters — the ONLY axis with a genuine swappable-vendor story, so the only one that earns a Protocol; mirrors CustomPositioningRegistry); editor → Block; formatting toolbar → Intent (text-formatting, composes droplist/popover); operation vocabulary → reuse InputEvent.inputType (native-first, no new vocab); serialization → Config-extends-platform-default registry (CustomSerializerRegistry, default-less core, HTML flavor default); paste/sanitization → standalone Plug (CustomSanitizerRegistry: native setHTML + DOMPurify adapter, composed on paste); decorations → CSS Custom Highlight API by default; collaboration → out of scope, cross-ref webrealtime (non-fungible, like #370 cross-ref'd sync-transport). 7 forks, near-ratification except Fork 2 (document-model Protocol, med) and the internal pivot model shape (tree vs flat-Delta, low/divergent — the #64-Fork-4 analogue). Report: reports/2026-06-14-rich-text-editing.md.

title: Domain-entity lifecycle — the transition map is a portable interchange, so the placement is Project + Protocol
summary: Grounds decision #616 (G3 governance finding from the #607 audit, slip->drift): retroactively ratify the placement #353 minted inline — a new WE project (weblifecycle, projects.json:253) owning a draft protocol (lifecycle, protocols.json:143) — which #353's own body left as an open question ('Project + Protocol, or an intent?') and which shipped ungoverned because the carve-rule landed in the same commit. Ratify-shipped-code, not greenfield. The placement test is internal precedent, codified verbatim by #409 (master-detail): the four exercise-app standards each became a Project+Protocol 'because each had a real contract — a provider seam (CustomLifecycleProvider, CustomAuditProvider) or an interchange schema (DecisionRecord)'; master-detail had none, so it stayed a plain intent ('not every gap is a project'). #467 applied the same lens to responsive layout. Run against lifecycle, the test passes on BOTH disjuncts: a swappable CustomLifecycleProvider registry AND a portable, data-defined transition map (status set + each transition: from->to + optional guard predicate + permitted actor/role) that an engine plugs in behind without touching the entity; every transition emits an observable { entity, from, to, actor, at } event — the composition seam for audit-trail (#357) and reporting. #409 already NAMED lifecycle as a legitimate Project+Protocol; #616 only supplies the missing explicit ratification. Prior-art survey confirms (does not reshape) the design: (1) a transition map IS a recognized interchange — W3C standardized statechart serialization as SCXML (2015): an engine parses the structure and 'lets the calling code decide what guards mean and what actions do', exactly the protocol's transition-map-is-the-only-lock split, portable across engines (Apache Commons SCXML/Java, SCION/web); (2) the swappable-engine premise is real — XState adheres to SCXML, BPM servers and hand-rolled switches are the other end of the swap; (3) the domain-entity framing is the textbook FSM use case ('the lifecycle of one thing in response to events' — permit application, claim, contract); (4) actor/role-on-transition is standard practice; (5) the boundary against process orchestration is principled — an FSM is in one state at a time while BPMN allows many, so a single-status domain lifecycle is a narrower, distinct concern. Classification: technical contract -> Protocol owned by a Project (UX half is the separate status-indicator intent, realizesIntent); CustomLifecycleProvider is a runtime-DI seam; authorization -> Web Guards, persistence -> entity's own concern, audit -> composes via the event (bias-to-separation honoured); separate from webstates (reactive primitives, projects.json:58 — a different altitude with a distinct contract shape). Two forks at DoR, both defaulting to ratify-as-shipped: (1) standalone project vs fold into webstates -> standalone (high); (2) Protocol vs intent for the contract -> Protocol (high). No reversal of #353. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-domain-entity-lifecycle-placement.md.

title: Workflow as a meta-intent — process orchestration is the sibling weblifecycle deliberately excluded; placement is Project + Protocol + Intent
summary: Grounds decision #634 — should WE recognize a workflow (a 'meta-intent'): smaller UX intents arranged in directed progression toward one goal (checkout = collect-address -> pay -> confirm), with threaded state, a completion criterion, and a current position? Greenfield call, no workflow concept exists today. The survey establishes: (1) a workflow is a missing THIRD composition mode beyond block-owned simultaneous (composesIntents) and cross-cutting (accessible-name) — directed/temporal composition whose atoms are intents; (2) the coherence invariant that distinguishes a workflow from co-located steps recurs across every system surveyed — threaded state + directed progression + a single completion criterion + one current position — mechanical enough to gate on; (3) the platform has NO native multi-step primitive: <form> is single-step, the WAI-ARIA APG has no wizard pattern, only aria-current='step' is native step vocabulary, so a flow is hand-assembled from Navigation API + View Transitions + form-associated CEs today; (4) the strongest meta-schema is the statechart (XState <-> W3C SCXML, the same engine family #616 cited) whose 7-operator kernel (sequence, branch/guard, nest, parallel, completion/final, threaded context, current-position) maps one-to-one onto a workflow, and whose serialized graph is a portable interchange — the transition-map-is-the-only-lock split that classifies the orchestration machinery as a Protocol; (5) workflow is the deliberate SIBLING of #616's domain-entity lifecycle, not its twin — #616's own survey drew the boundary ('an FSM is in one state at a time, whereas BPMN allows a workflow to be in many ... weblifecycle should not absorb a process-engine scope'), and they differ in subject (persisted entity vs ephemeral user traversal), cardinality (one state vs many), event (audit trail vs back/undo over a session) and persistence — so siblings over a shared SCXML transition-map form, per bias-to-separation; (6) the 'several valid ways' (wizard/stepper, single-page, conversational, board) are all coherent, so they are a configurable REGISTER dimension, not a fork — market keeps state-threading (RHF FormProvider, Formik) and visual sequencing (MUI Stepper, Ant Steps, Carbon ProgressIndicator) as separate seams, with Ant's wait/process/finish/error the cleanest status enum (avoid the name 'stepper' — collides with the numeric NumberField); (7) cross-system operator universality (statecharts/BPMN/GitHub Actions/Temporal/UI steppers) tiers cleanly — Tier 1 core (sequence, branch, nest, context, completion, position), Tier 2 common (parallel fork/join, back/undo, gate/wait-for-event), Tier 3 niche (fan-out, history, retry, compensation/saga, inclusive-OR). Classification by the #409 placement test: workflow passes on BOTH disjuncts (a swappable CustomWorkflowEngine registry AND a portable orchestration graph), so it decomposes like lifecycle — orchestration machinery = Protocol owned by a Project, UX (position/status/register) = Intent (UX-only, reusing navigation's structure:linear/guard/history), concrete wizard = Block composing step-intents. Two forks at DoR: (1) Placement -> Project+Protocol+Intent decomposition (high); (2) Operator scope -> Tier-1 core + Tier-2 optional, defer Tier-3, open meta-schema (med-high). Both follow the #085 'standardize the meta-schema, not the list' posture. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-workflow-meta-intent.md.

title: What is a block protocol — the runtime-agnostic contract for a UI block, decoupled from its implementation
summary: Grounds decision #641 (the blocks analogue of the #606 plugs ruling). WE's top-level blocks/ are byte-identical vendored copies of frontierui/blocks/ (e.g. navigation/NavListBehavior.ts diff-clean) — the same copy-paste hazard #170 flagged for plugs. #606 already settled the impl-home axis: implementation -> @frontierui (FUI owns the canonical code), WE keeps the CONTRACTS and consumes the impl as a #604 client; #606 explicitly names 'block protocols' among the contracts WE keeps. So #641's novel question is narrowly: WHAT IS a block protocol — what artifact in WE expresses a block's contract? Prior-art survey of how the ecosystem expresses a component/block contract decoupled from impl: (1) Custom Elements Manifest (CEM, custom-elements.json, community schema v2.1.0) — machine-readable JSON describing attributes/properties/events/slots/CSS-parts/custom-props/methods, the STRUCTURAL API contract, with a live analyzer + LSP + MCP + broad consumer ecosystem (IDEs, docs, catalogs, wrapper generators); shipped by Shoelace/Web Awesome, Carbon, Spectrum, Fluent. (2) Open UI (W3C CG) + WAI-ARIA APG — the BEHAVIORAL contract: anatomy -> parts -> states -> behaviors + role/state/keyboard tables + conformance tests, impl-independent and the platform's own way of specifying controls. (3) TS interface / .d.ts — weakest standards-alignment, language-specific, treated by the ecosystem as an INPUT the CEM analyzer reads, not the portable contract. The W3C UI Specification Schema CG (chartered Aug 2025 to define an implementation-agnostic JSON meta-model aligned to Open UI vocabulary) is direct evidence the JSON-meta-model target is recognized — but it CLOSED 21 May 2026, a cautionary signal about standing up a brand-new schema vs extending CEM. WE already has the contract surface today: src/_data/blocks.json entries (implementsIntent, exports, events, traits, sourcePath, webStandards, designDecisions) + src/_includes/block-descriptions/{id}.njk prose — a JSON descriptor + behavioral spec pair that maps cleanly onto (1)+(2). Load-bearing finding: a block protocol = the existing blocks.json entry (formalized as a CEM-aligned structural contract) + its block-description (the Open UI/APG behavioral spec), with sourcePath repointed from WE-owned vendored code to the canonical @frontierui/blocks impl. Three forks at DoR: (1) what the contract artifact IS, (2) impl-home + dedup mechanics (direct #606 analogue), (3) may a block protocol exist without a FUI impl (the WE-only families wrinkle). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-14-block-protocol-impl-boundary.md.

title: Reveal navigation menus — how leading sites do top-level dropdown / mega-menu / hover-disclosure (closed & open states)
summary: Live-navigation competitive sweep grounding the reveal-nav candidate standard #609. Drove headless Chromium to 8 public sites (Stripe, Vercel, GitHub, MDN, Apple, USWDS, GOV.UK, AWS), capturing the closed bar + opened panel and each trigger's a11y facts + reveal method. Recurring invariants: disclosure semantics (every trigger is a <button aria-expanded> toggling a panel of plain links — none use role=menu, per WAI-ARIA APG), overlay-no-reflow, aria-controls trigger->panel, Esc/outside-click dismissal + focus-return, sectioned mega columns. The central genuine fork handed to #609 is reveal trigger HOVER vs CLICK: both coherent, so a configurable dimension with click as the most-permissive default (touch-safe) and hover an author opt-in that MUST carry a tap-to-toggle fallback; panel width/anchor (full-width mega vs trigger-anchored dropdown) and density are open config dimensions, not forks. USWDS is the accessible-disclosure reference baseline; GOV.UK deliberately ships no desktop hover mega. Full matrix + handoff in reports/2026-06-14-design-sweep-navigation-menus.md.

title: Ejectable composition recipes — what a build-your-own-component assembler emits, and where it lives
summary: Prior-art survey grounding the assembler-shape decision #652 (under epic #646), the ergonomic home #609 named for pure-composition meta-components (nav-list + disclosure + anchor + hover-intent wired together, which earn no block). The question: what format a preset emits on eject, and where the tool sits relative to the #623-627 Web Docs catalog pipeline. The decisive precedent is shadcn/ui's distribution model — 'not a component library, a code-distribution platform': you copy plain source INTO your repo and own it (no runtime dependency), and a thin registry-item.json wrapper ({name, type:'registry:block', files:[{path,content}], registryDependencies}) is merely the CLI transport that CARRIES the code, never a replacement for it. Webstudio/Webflow/Builder.io corroborate (visual builders that export clean, host-anywhere HTML/CSS/JS to dodge lock-in); Lit/Web Components reinforce the no-runtime-framework invariant. The load-bearing reshaping: #652 Fork 1's framing of 'plain markup vs CEM-aligned descriptor vs bespoke recipe JSON' is a FALSE trichotomy — those three live on three orthogonal layers that COEXIST, not rival payloads. (1) The ejectable payload (what the author owns) = plain markup + WE declarative wiring — forced invariant under #609's constraints, the only option that is literally 'the standard, ejected'. (2) The API descriptor (optional export of the result) = CEM, already ratified-recommended as WE's component-API-metadata protocol by #626 — it describes the composed meta-component's API surface, never its internal wiring. (3) The distribution wrapper (how presets are stored/shared, the INPUT side) = a registry-item-shaped manifest carrying the payload — the same artifact that answers Fork 2's shared-registry question. So Fork 1 collapses to a forced invariant (plain markup+wiring) + support-all additional emit targets; the only genuinely rejected branch is a bespoke project-facing recipe JSON handed to the author INSTEAD of markup (the lock-in minimize-lock-in refuses, and shadcn shows it is unnecessary). Classification: the assembler is devtools (author-time tool, not a running-app seam) so zero lock-in / no protocol; the preset store is a registry it reads, sibling to workbenchFeatures.json/workbenchTools.json (one-capability-many-consumers); bias-toward-separation keeps the authoring tool distinct from the catalog/served-product surface (#627). Full grounding in reports/2026-06-15-assembler-recipe-emit-prior-art.md.

title: Self-Driven Project — SAE-style autonomy levels for the whole SDLC, the value/risk-as-ODD dial, and tool-agnostic everything-as-code
summary: Prior-art survey grounding the framing decision #665 (epic #666): the self-driving-car model applied to the whole software lifecycle — a human sets the goal + a tolerance/risk envelope, the system drives the steps gated/transparent/reversible, escalating only on tolerance breach. THREE findings reshape the forks. (1) SAE-levels-for-the-SDLC is ALREADY published convergently — ASDLC.io (L1 Assistive→L5 Full, borrowing SAE's own 'Conditional/High/Full' names), Dash0's six levels (with an explicit L3.5 'Selective Auto-Merge'), Tessl, and arXiv 2509.06216 which explicitly calls for 'a hierarchical framework analogous to the SAE Levels'. So the mapping is legitimate prior art to CITE, not originate; the ASDLC/Dash0 ladders number by a review/merge-gating axis (propose→human-review→auto-review→auto-merge→live) that lines up with the repo's own dev-browser ladder (#141: report-only→propose→live-verify→open-PR), whereas Swarmia numbers by agent-topology (single→swarm) and is NOT comparable. Notably NONE of the published ladders has an L0 'report-only' floor — that is a genuine addition. (2) The GENUINELY NOVEL contribution (not found in any surveyed standard/framework): partitioning EVERY lifecycle activity into value-creation vs risk-mitigation and using the risk side — described by named quality dimensions — as the per-activity AI-autonomy DIAL (the dimensions act as the SAE Operational Design Domain / tolerance envelope governing how far the AI drives each step). The substrate it stands on is precedented and citable: the dimensions map to ISO/IEC 25010:2023 (revised 8→9 characteristics, adding 'safety'; usability→interaction-capability, portability→flexibility; ISO calls them 'quality requirements', not NFRs), and the dimension list is best modeled as an OPEN, extensible registry with a default flavor (Config-Extends-Platform-Default) — strong precedent in FURPS+ (the '+' is literally an extension slot), SEI/ATAM (open quality-attribute scenarios, a set that grows edition to edition) and arc42 (a 184-quality default catalog tailored into a per-project quality tree); ISO's top level is fixed-per-revision but applied by selection/tailoring. (3) The methodology must be codified SEPARATELY from any tool we ship, as tool-agnostic everything-as-code, so a third-party project-management/CI tool could read and drive the SAME artefacts (no lock-in; our tools are non-blocking consumers). This is well-precedented as a pattern — GitOps names it ('declarative desired state in Git', OpenGitOps Principles v1.0.0: declarative/versioned-immutable/pulled/continuously-reconciled), and per-category neutral schemas prove the play (SARIF/OASIS for findings, SPDX/ISO-5962 & CycloneDX/OWASP for SBOM, OpenTelemetry/OPA/CloudEvents/CNCF, OSCAL/NIST for compliance-as-code) — but work-tracking/project-management data is a genuine WHITE SPACE: there is no adopted open work-item interchange standard (portability is lossy CSV, non-portable even GitLab CE↔EE), and the repo's own backlog-as-version-controlled-Markdown is docs-as-code applied to that gap. Naming caveats: 'self-driving' is strongly Oracle's (self-driving database) so lean on 'self-driven' and avoid 'self-driving database/infra/SDLC'; 'agentic SDLC' is the crowded generic category; and 'Project' already names a WE standard entity (projects.json), so the brand overloads an existing term — a sub-decision in #665 Fork 3. Classification rulings the survey settles: the autonomy ladder is a configured level (already ratified for the dev-loop in #141, default open-PR) not a new fork; the tool-agnostic artefact contract legitimately IS a Protocol (the one place a third-party tool must swap in — minimize-lock-in's single escapable lock); the dimensions are an open registry not a closed five; the non-technical control plane is plateau-app product. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-15-self-driven-project-prior-art.md.

title: Adapter-driven source form — a framework adapter emits its native dialect
summary: Codifies #663 (under the generation-adapter epic #507): the assembler/MaaS generation origin must NOT privilege HTML/declarative as THE source form — emitted source dialect is a capability the active framework adapter resolves. A plain-WC adapter emits HTML (declarative `<component>`), a React adapter emits TSX, a Vue adapter emits SFC; the MaaS FORMS catalog (blocks/renderers/module-service/moduleService.ts — declarative/wc-class/html/jsx/functional) becomes the open, multi-dialect set those adapters resolve rather than a closed enum that treats `html` as the privileged source. Two settled principles ground it: native-first (no framework adapter selected ⇒ plain WC → HTML, the web-platform-standard form; a framework dialect is the adapter author's opt-in, never the floor) and adapter-as-normalization-hub (the adapter owns the per-dialect transform). The card's one open dimension — a single canonical dialect per adapter vs one component served in MULTIPLE dialects with the adapter supplying the transform per dialect — resolves toward the MULTI-dialect framing per the most-flexible-default rule: the serve path already serves many forms off one definition, so exposing the whole axis (one component, many adapter-resolved dialects) is the most permissive end-state and the restriction would be the author's opt-in, not the floor. This feeds the assembler emit-format decision (#652/#646): #652 should treat the emitted recipe's dialect as adapter-driven, not bake HTML/JSX. Seam codified in the ServeForm doc-comment in moduleService.ts.

title: AI-assisted translation — the "AI proposes, the standard verifies" loop for webintl
summary: Grounds #664: an AI-assisted translation capability that turns a source-locale MF2 message catalog into target-locale catalogs, applying the settled vision-is-a-service ruling (#475) and the linear-cost on-device-floor pricing pattern (#488). The capability is a PLATEAU SERVICE the WE project consumes through a thin swappable client seam — NOT an @webeverything artifact — because translation is an implementation capability, not a standard (no-leakage: only the produced catalog reaches the standard). The one durable @webeverything edge is the VERIFICATION, now codified in the localization protocol contract: a produced catalog is provably-conformant (preserves every MF2 placeholder/selector/markup part, covers the target locale's CLDR plural/gender categories, no untranslated-but-claimed keys, no placeholder-arity drift). This is the #089 white-space loop — incumbents (Lokalise/Phrase/DeepL) produce plausible translations; ours produces provably-conformant ones because webintl gives a machine-checkable target. The pricing model is NOT re-opened: on-device (browser Translator/Language-Detector API + small open MT — NLLB-200/MADLAD-400/Opus-MT) is the bundled default/floor, hosted-API + BYO-key is the metered bridge for low-resource pairs and quality-sensitive copy, with per-locale-pair graduation benchmarks (#488 cadence). Three translation-specific open questions remain (research, not blocking forks): (1) which locale-pairs clear the on-device conformance bar today vs need the hosted bridge; (2) whether the (source, target, verdict) corpus becomes a distillation dataset for an on-device fine-tune the way the #488 vision verdict cache does; (3) catalog- vs message-level invocation and how MF2 markup parts survive a non-MF2-aware model (pre/post placeholder-masking in the client seam). The executable conformance harness is a deferred build that lands when the localization protocol graduates from `concept`.

title: Agentic-SDLC opinionated practices — each as a configurable dimension, not a mandate
summary: Grounds the composable/configurable thesis of the self-driven-project methodology (#690/#672/#703): a competitive survey of how the field runs agentic/autonomous software delivery, with every opinionated practice extracted and classified per the WE fork-existence + dimension-vs-fixed-mechanic tests into a configurable dimension (default = most-flexible), a fixed invariant, or a recipe-level house-style knob. Web-verified sources: ASDLC.io (micro-commits + merge-to-main human-accountability boundary), Tessl (spec-centric framework + Spec Registry), CodeRabbit (AI-review-in-loop with YAML custom rules), Swarmia (DORA/SPACE metrics + working agreements), and arXiv 2509.06216 (Hassan et al., SE 3.0 foundational pillars, ACE/AEE, Merge-Readiness/Consultation packs); GitOps/OpenGitOps, OSCAL, SPDX and arXiv 2510.19692 summarized from prior knowledge as candidate. The structural finding: almost nothing is a fixed invariant (only the everything-as-code foundation — declarative + version-controlled, the OpenGitOps floor — and gate-checks-outcome-not-authoring-order resist a knob); MOST field-mandated practices are not new dimensions but map onto WE's two existing axes — the autonomy-level ladder (#141/#690: merge boundaries, mandatory review, consultation packs, PR-vs-direct) or a value/risk-ODD tolerance (coverage/quality/metrics thresholds) — which is the strongest confirmation of #690's two-layer framing. The few genuinely-new dimensions are small (commit-granularity, branching-model, test-timing, squash/merge), each defaulting most-flexible; house-style items (commit-msg format, conventional commits) are recipe knobs. Feeds #672 Layer 1's autonomy-level registry + value/risk-ODD dimension registry + default-recipe knob values. No classification here is a blocking fork — they are defaults to ratify in #672.

title: Managed-inbox / auth-capture capability — build-vs-buy & the Plateau-service boundary
summary: Prep research for decision #709 (the boundary fork carved out of #611, the auth-walled app-screenshot capability). An agent that screenshots apps behind a login needs a managed email identity it controls end-to-end (create inboxes, read verification/magic/OTP mail, drive signup via Playwright, vault credentials). Two forks. Fork 1 — where the capability lives — is a near-exact replay of the #475 vision ruling: it is a stateful, credential-bearing IMPL capability, not a WE standard, so by #475 it is a Plateau service WE consumes as a NO-LEAKAGE client, with only a thin vendor-free client seam in WEs capture devtooling (the design-refs/vision.mjs precedent: env-selected provider module, repointed at the Plateau service when it exists) [recommended A — high confidence]; owning the capability inside the WE standards repo (B) rebuilds in WE what Plateau should own and drags credential-vaulting + signup automation into the standards layer (the broken branch). Fork 2 — build vs buy — is governed by Plateaus linear-cost-with-revenue hard rule: metered per-inbox/per-email SaaS (MailSlurp, Mailosaur) scales cost with capture volume not revenue, so it is excluded as the FLOOR and demoted to an opt-in BYO tier; the recommended floor is an owned-domain catch-all (Cloudflare Email Routing + Email Workers, free, fixed-cost; or a self-hosted Stalwart/maddy catch-all on the Plateau box) [med-high]. The exact owned realization is a Plateau implementation sub-choice (delegated to the Plateau build); WE states the floor and the constraint. Landscape note: dev SMTP sinks (Mailpit, Inbucket) are the WRONG tool — they capture your own outbound only, no public MX, so they cannot receive a target sites verification mail. Abuse/ToS guardrails (only sign up where terms permit, no CAPTCHA circumvention, respect rate limits, vault credentials) are required-by-default invariants, not a fork. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-15-managed-inbox-auth-capture.md.

title: Device-capacity provider — detection-axis + composite multi-source routing
summary: Prep research for decision #729 (an extension of the capability-provider epic #203). The provider seam models platform-FEATURE capability (Baseline/web-features tiers) and picks ONE provider per scope by venue; #729 asks it to (1) add a device-CAPACITY axis (hardwareConcurrency, deviceMemory, network/saveData, battery, GPU tier) and (2) combine providers so different checks route to different sources — delegating detection to existing solutions (WE standardizes the contract+routing, never the algorithm). The survey reshapes the item from two forks to THREE. Signal landscape with one load-bearing finding — availability is NOT uniform across venues: hardwareConcurrency + GPU-tier are runtime-only (no request header carries them), while deviceMemory (Sec-CH-Device-Memory) and Save-Data also resolve at the edge — so the capacity axis should REUSE the existing Venue dimension + the undefined-means-unknown degrade contract, an argument FOR a sibling provider on the same machinery. Battery is excluded not forked (deprecated, removed by Firefox, never in Safari, fingerprinting vector, ~76% Chromium-only) — the fork-existence test in action. Combinator prior-art: Google Chrome Labs react-adaptive-hooks (+ Addy Osmani's adaptive-loading pattern, svelte-adaptive-sensors port) combine exactly hardwareConcurrency+deviceMemory+effectiveType+saveData; WE's capacity provider is the platform-neutral, framework-free version. Every combinator consumes COARSE BUCKETS at the call site (effectiveType '4g', GPU tier 0–3) derived from a raw measurement — which is the new Fork 2 (expose both raw scalar AND derived bucket; default both). Only GPU-tier warrants a maintained dependency: detect-gpu (pmndrs — WebGL benchmark→tier 0–3, handles Apple privacy-masked renderer strings); CPU/RAM/network are thin native reads, battery excluded — delegation met with ONE lib. Recommended: Fork 1 separate CapacityProvider [high], Fork 2 both scalar+bucket [med-high], Fork 3 CompositeProvider routing by check-domain [high]. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-15-device-capacity-provider.md.

title: Embedded-demo overlay escape — how an iframed demo's modal covers the host page
summary: Prep research for decision #732 (a carved fork of the embed-mechanism epic #728). The fuiDemo shortcode (.eleventy.js:38) embeds a FUI-hosted demo in a sandboxed, fixed-height iframe (sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin', style='height:${h}px'; .fui-demo wrapper has overflow:hidden, style.css:1657). A demo's modal/popover/toast can't escape that box to cover the host docs page. The load-bearing finding is a PLATFORM CONSTRAINT, not an effort tradeoff: the top layer (<dialog> showModal / ::backdrop, the Popover API) is PER-DOCUMENT, so an iframe document's top layer is confined to the iframe's box — by security design, no iframe content can paint outside its frame. This is exactly the crux of the WE Dialog block (blocks.json:3286), whose thesis is 'native-first top-layer mount via <dialog> showModal + ::backdrop, never a position:fixed re-implementation' — so the Dialog family (modal/drawer/sheet) is the first #604 block that visibly breaks inside fuiDemo, the concrete trigger #732 asks for. Prior art reshaped the forks: the resize concern (auto-size the frame to content) is a SOLVED, de-facto-standard protocol — iframe-resizer (davidjbradshaw, v5; child posts content size via postMessage, parent resizes, MutationObserver/ResizeObserver-driven, child-initiated to bypass cross-origin) and oEmbed's real-world height-postMessage pattern (Twitter/Instagram ignore the spec's fixed height and post height instead) — so WE should REUSE that message shape, never mint a WE resize protocol (minimize-lock-in). The overlay-escape concern splits into TWO host-side flavors the original item collapsed into one 'B': B1 — host PROMOTES the existing frame to a fixed full-viewport layer (the Intercom messenger model: host toggles position:fixed;inset:0 on the frame; the demo paints its own backdrop, now covering everything; one boolean open/close message) vs B2 — host renders the backdrop/positioning NATIVELY (demo posts overlay intent+geometry; host paints its own <dialog>/popover, syncs geometry + focus-trap handoff). Net classification: auto-resize rides along as a baseline (strictly better than fixed pixel heights across themes/viewports) once any channel exists; origin-validated postMessage is a forced invariant (the payment-skimmer overlay/postMessage-spoof attack surface, per The Hacker News 2025); the overlay message contract stays shortcode-local/unregistered until a SECOND embed consumer appears (per #728's own slicing ruling — component.njk:235 is the only consumer today), then registers as a webembed-style protocol. The genuine call is the escape mechanism: recommended A-contained baseline + B1-promote on opt-in [med — B1 vs B2 is where judgment lives], B2 rejected as over-built for a 'see it run' demo, C (defer to the #700-gated DI mount) unavailable today. Full grounding in reports/2026-06-16-iframe-overlay-escape.md.
